title: Brooklyn, New York

infobox.value: [Antonio Reynoso](/wiki/Antonio_Reynoso "Antonio Reynoso") ([D](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"))  — *(Borough of Brooklyn)*

[7th](/wiki/New_York%27s_7th_congressional_district "New York's 7th congressional district"), [8th](/wiki/New_York%27s_8th_congressional_district "New York's 8th congressional district"), [9th](/wiki/New_York%27s_9th_congressional_district "New York's 9th congressional district"), [10th](/wiki/New_York%27s_10th_congressional_district "New York's 10th congressional district"), [11th](/wiki/New_York%27s_11th_congressional_district "New York's 11th congressional district")

is the most populous of the five [boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"), coextensive with **Kings County**, in the [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). Located at the westernmost end of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") and formerly an independent city, Brooklyn shares a land border with the borough and county of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"). It has several bridge and tunnel connections to the borough of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), including the architecturally significant [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), and is connected to [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") by the [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge").

**Brooklyn** is the most populous of the five [boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"), coextensive with **Kings County**, in the [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") [state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"). Located at the westernmost end of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") and formerly an independent city, Brooklyn shares a land border with the borough and county of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"). It has several bridge and tunnel connections to the borough of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), including the architecturally significant [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), and is connected to [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") by the [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge").

The borough, as Kings County, at 37,339.9 inhabitants per square mile (14,417.0/km2), is the second [most densely populated county](/wiki/County_statistics_of_the_United_States#Most_densely_populated "County statistics of the United States") in the U.S. after Manhattan (New York County), and the most populous [county](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#County "Administrative divisions of New York (state)") in the state, as of 2022.[[8]](#cite_note-CensusDensity2022-8) In the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[3]](#cite_note-2020CensusMap-3) the borough had a population of 2,736,074.[[9]](#cite_note-Counties2020NYS-9)[[10]](#cite_note-GR1-10)[[11]](#cite_note-CensusPopulation2022-11) Had Brooklyn remained an independent city on Long Island, it would now be the [fourth most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") American city after the rest of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), and [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago").[[11]](#cite_note-CensusPopulation2022-11) With a land area of 69.38 square miles (179.7 km2) and a water area of 27.48 square miles (71.2 km2), Kings County, one of the twelve original counties established under British rule in 1683 in the then-[province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York"), is the state of New York's [fourth-smallest county](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_York "List of counties in New York") by land area and third smallest by total area.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Brooklyn, named after the Dutch town of [Breukelen](/wiki/Breukelen "Breukelen") in the Netherlands, was founded by [the Dutch](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland") in the 17th century and grew into a busy port city on [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") by the 19th century. On January 1, 1898, after a long political campaign and public-relations battle during the 1890s and despite opposition from Brooklyn residents, Brooklyn was consolidated and annexed, along with other areas, to form the current five-borough structure of New York City in accordance with the new municipal charter of [Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York").[[13]](#cite_note-13) The borough continues to maintain [a distinct culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Brooklyn "Culture of Brooklyn"). Many [Brooklyn neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Brooklyn_neighborhoods "List of Brooklyn neighborhoods") are [ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Ethnic_enclave "Ethnic enclave"). With [Jews forming around a fifth of its population](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City"), the borough has been described as one of the main global hubs for Jewish culture.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Brooklyn's official motto, displayed on the borough seal and [flag](/wiki/Flags_of_New_York_City%27s_boroughs#Brooklyn "Flags of New York City's boroughs"), is *Een Draght Mackt Maght*, which translates from early modern [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") as "[In unity there is strength](/wiki/Unity_makes_strength "Unity makes strength")".[[1]](#cite_note-Motto-1)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Educational institutions in Brooklyn include the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York")'s [Brooklyn College](/wiki/Brooklyn_College "Brooklyn College"), [Medgar Evers College](/wiki/Medgar_Evers_College "Medgar Evers College"), and [College of Technology](/wiki/New_York_City_College_of_Technology "New York City College of Technology"), as well as [Pratt Institute](/wiki/Pratt_Institute "Pratt Institute"), [Long Island University](/wiki/LIU_Brooklyn "LIU Brooklyn"), and the [New York University Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering"). In sports, basketball's [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets"), and [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") play at the [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center"). In the first decades of the 21st century, Brooklyn has experienced a renaissance as a destination for [hipsters](/wiki/Hipster_(contemporary_subculture) "Hipster (contemporary subculture)"),[[16]](#cite_note-16) with associated [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), dramatic house-price increases, and a decrease in housing affordability.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Some new developments are now required to include affordable housing units.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Since the 2010s, parts of Brooklyn have evolved into a hub for entrepreneurship, high-technology [startup firms](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company"),[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-BrooklynDesignHub-20) [postmodern art](/wiki/Postmodern_art "Postmodern art"),[[21]](#cite_note-BrooklynArt1-21) and design.[[20]](#cite_note-BrooklynDesignHub-20)

## Toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Toponymy")]

The name Brooklyn is derived from the original [Dutch](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") town of [Breukelen](/wiki/Breukelen "Breukelen").[[22]](#cite_note-22) This form is made up of the words *broeck*, meaning bog or [marshland](/wiki/Marshland "Marshland"), and *lede*, meaning small (dug) water stream, specifically in peat areas.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Breuckelen on the American continent was established in 1646, and the name first appeared in print in 1663.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Over the past two millennia, the name of the ancient town in Holland has been *Bracola*, *Broccke*, *Brocckede*, *Broiclede*, *Brocklandia*, *Broekclen*, *Broikelen*, *Breuckelen*, and finally *Breukelen*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The New Amsterdam settlement of *Breuckelen* also went through many spelling variations, including *Breucklyn*, *Breuckland*, *Brucklyn*, *Broucklyn*, *Brookland*, *Brockland*, *Brocklin*, and *Brookline/Brook-line*. There have been so many variations of the name that its origin has been debated; some have claimed *breuckelen* means "broken land".[[28]](#cite_note-28) The current name, however, is the one that best reflects its meaning.[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The county's name, **Kings County**, was named after [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") of England, who ruled from 1660 to 1685.

The history of [European settlement](/wiki/European_colonization_of_the_Americas "European colonization of the Americas") in Brooklyn spans more than 350 years. The settlement began in the 17th century as the small [Dutch-founded](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") town of "Breuckelen" on the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") shore of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), grew to be a sizeable city in the 19th century and was consolidated in 1898 with New York City (then confined to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx")), the remaining rural areas of Kings County, and the largely rural areas of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), to form the modern City of New York.

### Colonial era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Colonial era")]

#### New Netherland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: New Netherland")]

The [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") were the first Europeans to settle Long Island's western edge, which was then largely inhabited by the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"), an Algonquian-speaking [American Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribe often referred to in European documents by a variation of the place name "[Canarsie](/wiki/Canarsie "Canarsie")". Bands were associated with place names, but the colonists thought their names represented different tribes. The *Breuckelen* settlement was named after [Breukelen](/wiki/Breukelen "Breukelen") in the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"); it was part of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"). The [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company") lost little time in chartering the six original parishes (listed here by their later English town names):[[31]](#cite_note-31)

* [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend,_Brooklyn "Gravesend, Brooklyn"): in 1645, settled under Dutch patent by English followers of [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") [Deborah Moody](/wiki/Deborah_Moody "Deborah Moody"), named for ['s-Gravenzande](/wiki/%27s-Gravenzande "'s-Gravenzande"), Netherlands, or [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend "Gravesend"), England;
* [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights,_Brooklyn "Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn"): chartered as *Breuckelen* in 1646, after the town now spelled [Breukelen](/wiki/Breukelen "Breukelen"), Netherlands. Breuckelen was along Fulton Street (now Fulton Mall) between Hoyt Street and Smith Street (according to H. Stiles and P. Ross). Brooklyn Heights, or Clover Hill, is where the village of Brooklyn was founded in 1816;
* [Flatlands](/wiki/Flatlands,_Brooklyn "Flatlands, Brooklyn"): chartered as *Nieuw [Amersfoort](/wiki/Amersfoort "Amersfoort")* in 1647;
* [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush,_Brooklyn "Flatbush, Brooklyn"): chartered as *[Midwout](/wiki/Midwood,_Brooklyn "Midwood, Brooklyn")* in 1652;
* [*Nieuw Utrecht*](/wiki/New_Utrecht,_Brooklyn "New Utrecht, Brooklyn") in 1652, named after the city of [Utrecht](/wiki/Utrecht_(city) "Utrecht (city)"), Netherlands; and
* [Bushwick](/wiki/Bushwick,_Brooklyn "Bushwick, Brooklyn"): chartered as *Boswijck* in 1661.

The colony's capital of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), across the East River, obtained its charter in 1653. The neighborhood of [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park,_Brooklyn "Marine Park, Brooklyn") was home to North America's first [tide mill](/wiki/Tide_mill "Tide mill"). It was built by the Dutch, and the foundation can be seen today. But the area was not formally settled as a town. Many incidents and documents relating to this period are in [Gabriel Furman](/wiki/Gabriel_Furman "Gabriel Furman")'s 1824 compilation.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

#### Province of New York

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Province of New York")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/BrooklynMap1766.jpg/330px-BrooklynMap1766.jpg)](/wiki/File:BrooklynMap1766.jpg)

Village of Brooklyn and environs, 1766

Present-day Brooklyn left Dutch hands after the English [conquest of New Netherland](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_Netherland "Conquest of New Netherland") in 1664, which sparked the [Second Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Dutch_War "Second Anglo-Dutch War"). New Netherland was taken in a naval action, and the English renamed the new capture for their naval commander, [James, Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"), brother of the then monarch [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") and future king himself as [King James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"). Brooklyn became a part of the West Riding of [York Shire](/wiki/York_Shire_(Province_of_New_York) "York Shire (Province of New York)") in the [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York"), one of the [Middle Colonies](/wiki/Middle_Colonies "Middle Colonies") in [England's North American colonies](/wiki/British_America "British America").

On November 1, 1683, *Kings County* was partitioned from the West Riding of York Shire, containing the six old Dutch towns on southwestern Long Island,[[33]](#cite_note-33) as one of the ["original twelve counties"](/wiki/Province_of_New_York#Counties "Province of New York"). This tract of land was recognized as a political entity for the first time, and the municipal groundwork was laid for a later expansive idea of a Brooklyn identity.

Lacking the [patroon](/wiki/Patroon "Patroon") and tenant farmer system established along the [Hudson River Valley](/wiki/Hudson_Valley "Hudson Valley"), this [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") county unusually came to have one of the highest percentages of [slaves](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_colonial_United_States "Slavery in the colonial United States") among the population in the ["Original Thirteen Colonies"](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies") along the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") eastern coast of [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America").[[34]](#cite_note-34)

#### Revolutionary War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Revolutionary War")]

Further information: [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island") and [New York and New Jersey campaign](/wiki/New_York_and_New_Jersey_campaign "New York and New Jersey campaign")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Battle-of-Long-Island-Map-sml.jpg/330px-Battle-of-Long-Island-Map-sml.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle-of-Long-Island-Map-sml.jpg)

The American Revolution's [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island") was fought across Kings County.

On August 27, 1776, the [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island") (also known as the 'Battle of Brooklyn') was fought, the first major engagement fought in the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") after [independence was declared](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), and the largest of the entire conflict. [British troops](/wiki/British_Army_during_the_American_Revolutionary_War "British Army during the American Revolutionary War") forced the [Continental Army](/wiki/Continental_Army "Continental Army") under [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") off the heights near the modern sites of [Green-Wood Cemetery](/wiki/Green-Wood_Cemetery "Green-Wood Cemetery"), [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)"), and [Grand Army Plaza](/wiki/Grand_Army_Plaza "Grand Army Plaza").[[35]](#cite_note-McCullough,_David_2005-35)

Washington, viewing particularly fierce fighting at the [Gowanus Creek](/wiki/Gowanus_Canal "Gowanus Canal") and [Old Stone House](/wiki/Old_Stone_House_(Brooklyn) "Old Stone House (Brooklyn)") from atop a hill near the west end of present-day [Atlantic Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Atlantic Avenue (Brooklyn)"), was reported to have emotionally exclaimed: "What brave men I must this day lose!".[[35]](#cite_note-McCullough,_David_2005-35)

The [fortified](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") American positions at [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights,_Brooklyn "Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn") consequently became untenable and were evacuated a few days later, leaving the British in control of [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"). While Washington's defeat on the battlefield cast early doubts on his ability as the commander, the [tactical withdrawal](/wiki/Tactical_withdrawal "Tactical withdrawal") of all his troops and supplies across the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") in a single night is now seen by historians as one of his most brilliant triumphs.[[35]](#cite_note-McCullough,_David_2005-35)

The British controlled the surrounding region for the duration of the war, as New York City was soon occupied and became their military and political base of operations in British-held North America for the remainder of the conflict. The [Patriot](/wiki/Patriot_(American_Revolution) "Patriot (American Revolution)") residents largely fled or changed their political sentiments, and afterward the British generally enjoyed a dominant [Loyalist](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)") sentiment from the residents in Kings County who did not evacuate, though the region was also the center of the fledgling—and largely successful—[Patriot intelligence network](/wiki/Intelligence_in_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Intelligence in the American Revolutionary War"), headed by Washington himself.

The British set up a system of [prison ships](/wiki/Prisoners_of_war_in_the_American_Revolutionary_War "Prisoners of war in the American Revolutionary War") off the coast of Brooklyn in [Wallabout Bay](/wiki/Wallabout_Bay "Wallabout Bay"). More American prisoners of war died on these prison ships than were [killed in action](/wiki/Killed_in_action "Killed in action") on all the battlefield engagements of the war combined. One result of the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1783) "Treaty of Paris (1783)") in 1783 was the [evacuation of the British from New York City](/wiki/Evacuation_Day_(New_York) "Evacuation Day (New York)"), which was celebrated by New Yorkers into the 20th century.

### Post-independence era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Post-independence era")]

#### Urbanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Urbanization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Francis_Guy_-_Winter_Scene_in_Brooklyn_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/330px-Francis_Guy_-_Winter_Scene_in_Brooklyn_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Francis_Guy_-_Winter_Scene_in_Brooklyn_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Winter Scene in Brooklyn*, c. 1819–20, by [Francis Guy](/wiki/Francis_Guy "Francis Guy") ([Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum"))

The first half of the 19th century saw the beginning of the development of urban areas on the economically strategic East River shore of Kings County, facing the adolescent City of New York confined to Manhattan Island. The [New York Navy Yard](/wiki/New_York_Naval_Shipyard "New York Naval Shipyard") operated in [Wallabout Bay](/wiki/Wallabout_Bay "Wallabout Bay") (border between Fort Greene and Williamsburg) during the 19th century and two-thirds of the 20th century.

The first center of [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization") sprang up in the Town of Brooklyn, directly across from [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), which saw the incorporation of the Village of Brooklyn in 1816. Reliable steam [ferry service](/wiki/List_of_ferries_across_the_East_River "List of ferries across the East River") across the East River to [Fulton Landing](/wiki/Fulton_Ferry,_Brooklyn "Fulton Ferry, Brooklyn") converted [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights,_Brooklyn "Brooklyn Heights, Brooklyn") into a [commuter town](/wiki/Commuter_town "Commuter town") for [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"). Ferry Road to Jamaica Pass became [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Brooklyn) "Fulton Street (Brooklyn)") to [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York "East New York"). Town and Village were combined to form the first, kernel incarnation of the City of Brooklyn in 1834.

In a parallel development, the Town of Bushwick, farther up the river, saw the incorporation of the Village of [Williamsburgh](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn") in 1827, which separated as the Town of Williamsburgh in 1840 and formed the short-lived City of Williamsburgh in 1851. [Industrial deconcentration](/wiki/Industrial_deconcentration "Industrial deconcentration") in the mid-century was bringing shipbuilding and other manufacturing to the northern part of the county. Each of the two cities and six towns in Kings County remained independent municipalities and purposely created non-aligning street grids with different naming systems.

However, the East River shore was growing too fast for the three-year-old infant City of Williamsburg; it, along with its Town of [Bushwick](/wiki/Bushwick,_Brooklyn "Bushwick, Brooklyn") hinterland, was subsumed within a greater City of Brooklyn in 1855, subsequently dropping the 'h' from its name.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

By 1841, with the appearance of *[The Brooklyn Eagle, and Kings County Democrat](/wiki/Brooklyn_Eagle "Brooklyn Eagle")* published by Alfred G. Stevens, the growing city across the East River from Manhattan was producing its own prominent newspaper.[[37]](#cite_note-37) It later became the most popular and highest circulation afternoon paper in America.[[38]](#cite_note-38) The publisher changed to L. Van Anden on April 19, 1842,[[39]](#cite_note-39) and the paper was renamed *The Brooklyn Daily Eagle and Kings County Democrat* on June 1, 1846.[[40]](#cite_note-40) On May 14, 1849, the name was shortened to *The Brooklyn Daily Eagle*;[[41]](#cite_note-41) on September 5, 1938, it was further shortened to *Brooklyn Eagle*.[[42]](#cite_note-42) The establishment of the paper in the 1840s helped develop a separate identity for Brooklynites over the next century. The borough's soon-to-be-famous [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") baseball team, the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers"), also assisted with this. Both major institutions were lost in the 1950s: the paper closed in 1955 after unsuccessful attempts at a sale following a reporters' strike, and the baseball team decamped for Los Angeles in a realignment of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") in 1957.

Agitation against [Southern](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States") was stronger in Brooklyn than in New York,[[43]](#cite_note-43) and under Republican leadership, the city was fervent in the Union cause in the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). After the war the [Henry Ward Beecher Monument](/wiki/Henry_Ward_Beecher_Monument "Henry Ward Beecher Monument") was built downtown to honor a famous local [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States"). A great victory arch was built at what was then the south end of town to celebrate the armed forces; this place is now called [Grand Army Plaza](/wiki/Grand_Army_Plaza "Grand Army Plaza").

The number of people living in Brooklyn grew rapidly early in the 19th century. There were 4,402 by 1810, 7,175 in 1820 and 15,396 by 1830.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The city's population was 25,000 in 1834, but the police department comprised only 12 men on the day shift and another 12 on the night shift. Every time a rash of burglaries broke out, officials blamed burglars from New York City. Finally, in 1855, a modern police force was created, employing 150 men. Voters complained of inadequate protection and excessive costs. In 1857, the state legislature merged the Brooklyn force with that of New York City.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Civil War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Civil War")]

Fervent in the Union cause, the city of Brooklyn played a major role in supplying troops and [materiel](/wiki/Materiel "Materiel") for the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). The best-known regiment to be sent off to war from the city was the [14th Brooklyn](/wiki/14th_Brooklyn "14th Brooklyn") *"Red Legged Devils"*. They fought from 1861 to 1864, wore red the entire war, and were the only regiment named after a city. President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") called them into service, making them part of a handful of three-year enlisted soldiers in April 1861. Unlike other regiments during the American Civil War, the 14th wore a uniform inspired by the French [Chasseurs](/wiki/Chasseur "Chasseur"), a light infantry used for quick assaults.

As a seaport and a manufacturing center, Brooklyn was well prepared to contribute to the Union's strengths in shipping and manufacturing. The two combined in shipbuilding; the ironclad *[Monitor](/wiki/USS_Monitor "USS Monitor")* was built in Brooklyn.

#### Twin city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Twin city")]

Brooklyn is referred to as the twin city of New York in the 1883 poem, "[The New Colossus](/wiki/The_New_Colossus "The New Colossus")" by [Emma Lazarus](/wiki/Emma_Lazarus "Emma Lazarus"), which appears on a plaque inside the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"). The poem calls New York Harbor "the air-bridged harbor that twin cities frame". As a twin city to New York, it played a role in national affairs that was later overshadowed by decades of subordination by its old partner and rival.

During this period, the affluent, contiguous districts of [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn") and [Clinton Hill](/wiki/Clinton_Hill,_Brooklyn "Clinton Hill, Brooklyn") (then characterized collectively as The Hill) were home to such notable figures as [Astral Oil Works](/wiki/Astral_Oil_Works "Astral Oil Works") founder [Charles Pratt](/wiki/Charles_Pratt "Charles Pratt") and his children, including local civic leader [Charles Millard Pratt](/wiki/Charles_Millard_Pratt "Charles Millard Pratt"); [Theosophical Society](/wiki/Theosophical_Society "Theosophical Society") co-founder [William Quan Judge](/wiki/William_Quan_Judge "William Quan Judge"); and [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer") co-founders [Charles Pfizer](/wiki/Charles_Pfizer "Charles Pfizer") and [Charles F. Erhart](/wiki/Charles_F._Erhart "Charles F. Erhart"). Brooklyn Heights remained one of the New York metropolitan area's most august patrician redoubts into the early 20th century under the aegis of such figures as abolitionist clergyman [Henry Ward Beecher](/wiki/Henry_Ward_Beecher "Henry Ward Beecher"), [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist") [theologians](/wiki/Theologian "Theologian") [Lyman Abbott](/wiki/Lyman_Abbott "Lyman Abbott") and [Newell Dwight Hillis](/wiki/Newell_Dwight_Hillis "Newell Dwight Hillis") (who followed Beecher as the second and third pastors of [Plymouth Church](/wiki/Plymouth_Church_(Brooklyn) "Plymouth Church (Brooklyn)"), respectively), financier [John Jay Pierrepont](/wiki/John_Jay_Pierrepont "John Jay Pierrepont") (a grandson of founding Heights resident [Hezekiah Pierrepont](/wiki/Hezekiah_Pierrepont "Hezekiah Pierrepont")), banker/art collector [David Leavitt](/wiki/David_Leavitt_(banker) "David Leavitt (banker)"), educator/politician [Seth Low](/wiki/Seth_Low "Seth Low"), merchant/banker [Horace Brigham Claflin](/wiki/Horace_Brigham_Claflin "Horace Brigham Claflin"), attorney [William Cary Sanger](/wiki/William_Cary_Sanger "William Cary Sanger") (who served for two years as [United States Assistant Secretary of War](/wiki/United_States_Assistant_Secretary_of_War "United States Assistant Secretary of War") under Presidents [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley") and [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt")) and publisher [Alfred Smith Barnes](/wiki/Alfred_Smith_Barnes "Alfred Smith Barnes"). Contiguous to the Heights, the less exclusive [South Brooklyn](/wiki/South_Brooklyn "South Brooklyn") was home to longtime civic leader [James S. T. Stranahan](/wiki/James_S._T._Stranahan "James S. T. Stranahan"), who became known (often derisively) as the "[Baron Haussmann](/wiki/Georges-Eug%C3%A8ne_Haussmann "Georges-Eugène Haussmann") of Brooklyn" for championing [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)") and other public works.

Economic growth continued, propelled by [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") and [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), and Brooklyn established itself as the third-most populous American city for much of the 19th century. The waterfront from [Gowanus](/wiki/Gowanus,_Brooklyn "Gowanus, Brooklyn") to [Greenpoint](/wiki/Greenpoint,_Brooklyn "Greenpoint, Brooklyn") was developed with piers and factories. Industrial access to the waterfront was improved by the [Gowanus Canal](/wiki/Gowanus_Canal "Gowanus Canal") and the canalized [Newtown Creek](/wiki/Newtown_Creek "Newtown Creek"). [USS *Monitor*](/wiki/USS_Monitor "USS Monitor") was the most famous product of the large and growing [shipbuilding industry](/wiki/Shipbuilding_industry "Shipbuilding industry") of Williamsburg. After the [Civil War](/wiki/US_Civil_War "US Civil War"), trolley lines and other transport brought [urban sprawl](/wiki/Urban_sprawl "Urban sprawl") beyond Prospect Park (completed by [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted") and [Calvert Vaux](/wiki/Calvert_Vaux "Calvert Vaux") in 1873 and widely heralded as an improvement upon the earlier [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park")) into the center of the county, as evinced by gradual settlement in the comparatively rustic villages of [Windsor Terrace](/wiki/Windsor_Terrace,_Brooklyn "Windsor Terrace, Brooklyn") and [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_Brooklyn "Kensington, Brooklyn") in the Town of Flatbush. By century's end, [Dean Alvord](/wiki/Dean_Alvord "Dean Alvord")'s [Prospect Park South](/wiki/Prospect_Park_South "Prospect Park South") development (adjacent to the village of Flatbush) would serve as the template for contemporaneous "[Victorian Flatbush](/wiki/Victorian_Flatbush "Victorian Flatbush")" micro-neighborhoods and the post-consolidation emergence of outlying districts, such as [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood,_Brooklyn "Midwood, Brooklyn") and [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park,_Brooklyn "Marine Park, Brooklyn"). Along with [Oak Park, Illinois](/wiki/Oak_Park,_Illinois "Oak Park, Illinois"), it also presaged the [automobile](/wiki/History_of_the_automobile "History of the automobile") and [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail")-driven vogue for more remote prewar suburban communities, such as [Garden City, New York](/wiki/Garden_City,_New_York "Garden City, New York") and [Montclair, New Jersey](/wiki/Montclair,_New_Jersey "Montclair, New Jersey").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Currier_and_Ives_Brooklyn_Bridge2.jpg/330px-Currier_and_Ives_Brooklyn_Bridge2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Currier_and_Ives_Brooklyn_Bridge2.jpg)

[Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") in 1883, by [Currier and Ives](/wiki/Currier_and_Ives "Currier and Ives")

The rapidly growing population needed more water, so the City built centralized waterworks, including the [Ridgewood Reservoir](/wiki/Ridgewood_Reservoir "Ridgewood Reservoir"). The municipal Police Department, however, was abolished in 1854 in favor of a Metropolitan force covering also New York and Westchester Counties. In 1865 the [Brooklyn Fire Department](/wiki/Brooklyn_Fire_Department "Brooklyn Fire Department") (BFD) also gave way to the new Metropolitan Fire District.

Throughout this period the peripheral towns of Kings County, far from Manhattan and even from urban Brooklyn, maintained their rustic independence. The only municipal change seen was the secession of the eastern section of the Town of Flatbush as the Town of New Lots in 1852. The building of [rail links](/wiki/History_of_the_New_York_City_Subway "History of the New York City Subway") such as the [Brighton Beach Line](/wiki/BMT_Brighton_Line "BMT Brighton Line") in 1878 heralded the end of this isolation.

[Sports in Brooklyn](/wiki/Sports_in_Brooklyn "Sports in Brooklyn") became a business. The Brooklyn Bridegrooms played professional baseball at Washington Park in the convenient suburb of [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope,_Brooklyn "Park Slope, Brooklyn") and elsewhere. Early in the next century, under their new name of Brooklyn Dodgers, they brought baseball to [Ebbets Field](/wiki/Ebbets_Field "Ebbets Field"), beyond Prospect Park. Racetracks, [amusement parks](/wiki/Amusement_park "Amusement park"), and [beach resorts](/wiki/Beach_resort "Beach resort") opened in [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach "Brighton Beach"), [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), and elsewhere in the southern part of the county.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Currier_%26_Ives_Brooklyn2.jpg/330px-Currier_%26_Ives_Brooklyn2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Currier_%26_Ives_Brooklyn2.jpg)

[Currier and Ives](/wiki/Currier_and_Ives "Currier and Ives") print of Brooklyn, 1886

Toward the end of the 19th century, the City of Brooklyn experienced its final, explosive growth spurt. Park Slope was rapidly urbanized, with its eastern summit soon emerging as the city's third "Gold Coast" district alongside Brooklyn Heights and The Hill; notable residents of the era included [American Chicle Company](/wiki/American_Chicle_Company "American Chicle Company") co-founder Thomas Adams Jr. and [New York Central Railroad](/wiki/New_York_Central_Railroad "New York Central Railroad") executive Clinton L. Rossiter. East of The Hill, [Bedford-Stuyvesant](/wiki/Bedford-Stuyvesant,_Brooklyn "Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn") coalesced as an upper middle class enclave for lawyers, shopkeepers, and merchants of German and Irish descent (notably exemplified by John C. Kelley, a water meter magnate and close friend of President [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland")), with nearby [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn") gradually fulfilling an analogous role for the city's Jewish population as development continued through the early 20th century. Northeast of Bedford-Stuyvesant, Bushwick (by now a working class, predominantly German district) established a considerable [brewery](/wiki/Brewery "Brewery") industry; the so-called "Brewer's Row" encompassed 14 breweries operating in a 14-block area in 1890. On the southwestern waterfront of Kings County, railroads and industrialization spread to [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn") (then coterminous with the city's sprawling, sparsely populated Eighth Ward) and adjacent [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn") (hitherto a resort-like subsection of the Town of [New Utrecht](/wiki/New_Utrecht "New Utrecht")). Within a decade, the city had annexed the Town of [New Lots](/wiki/New_Lots,_Brooklyn "New Lots, Brooklyn") in 1886; the Towns of [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush,_Brooklyn "Flatbush, Brooklyn"), [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend,_Brooklyn "Gravesend, Brooklyn") and New Utrecht in 1894; and the Town of [Flatlands](/wiki/Flatlands,_Brooklyn "Flatlands, Brooklyn") in 1896. Brooklyn had reached its natural municipal boundaries at the ends of Kings County.

##### Seth Low as mayor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Seth Low as mayor")]

Low's time in office from 1882 to 1885 was marked by a number of reforms:[[46]](#cite_note-Kurland-46)

* Secured a degree of "home rule" of the city. Previously, the State Government dictated city policies, hiring, salaries, and other affairs. Low managed to secure an unofficial veto over all Brooklyn bills in the State Assembly.
* Instituted a number of educational reforms. He was the first to integrate Brooklyn schools. He introduced free textbooks for all students, not just those who had taken a pauper's oath. He instituted a competitive examination for hiring teachers, instead of giving teaching jobs to pay political debts. He set aside $430,000 (equivalent to $14,345,690 in 2025) for the construction of new schools to accommodate 10,000 new students.
* Introduced Civil Service Code to all city employees, eliminating patronage jobs.
* [German Americans](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") wanted to enjoy their local beer gardens on the Sabbath, in violation of state "dry" laws and the demands of local puritanical clergy. Low's compromise solution was that saloons could stay open as long as they were orderly. At the first sign of rowdiness, they would be closed.
* Served as a member of the board of the New York Bridge Company, the company that built the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), and led an unsuccessful effort to remove [Washington Roebling](/wiki/Washington_Roebling "Washington Roebling") as the chief engineer on that project.[[47]](#cite_note-GreatBridge-47)
* Raised the tax rate from 2.33% of $100 assessed valuation in 1881 to 2.59% in 1883.[[46]](#cite_note-Kurland-46) He also went after property owners who had not paid back taxes. This increase in city revenue enabled him to reduce the city's debt and increase services. However, raising taxes proved extremely unpopular.

##### Mayors of the City of Brooklyn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Mayors of the City of Brooklyn")]

See also: [List of mayors of New York City](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_New_York_City "List of mayors of New York City") and [Brooklyn borough presidents](/wiki/Borough_President#Brooklyn_Borough_Presidents "Borough President")

Brooklyn elected a mayor from 1834 until 1898, after which it was consolidated into the [City of Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York"), the second mayor of which (1902–1903), Seth Low, had been Mayor of Brooklyn from 1882 to 1885. Since 1898, Brooklyn has, in place of a separate mayor, elected a [Borough President](/wiki/Borough_President "Borough President").

### New York City borough

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: New York City borough")]

Further information: [History of New York City (1898–1945)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1898%E2%80%931945) "History of New York City (1898–1945)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/1897_Brooklyn_map.jpg/330px-1897_Brooklyn_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:1897_Brooklyn_map.jpg)

Brooklyn in 1897

In 1883, the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") was completed, transportation to Manhattan was no longer by water only, and the City of Brooklyn's ties to the City of New York were strengthened.

The question became whether Brooklyn was prepared to engage in the still-grander process of consolidation then developing throughout the region, whether to join with the county of [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond_County,_New_York "Richmond County, New York") and the western portion of [Queens County](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), and the county of [New York](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), which by then already included [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), to form the five boroughs of a united City of New York. [Andrew Haswell Green](/wiki/Andrew_Haswell_Green "Andrew Haswell Green") and other progressives said yes, and eventually, they prevailed against the *[Daily Eagle](/wiki/Brooklyn_Eagle "Brooklyn Eagle")* and other conservative forces. In 1894, residents of Brooklyn and the other counties voted by a slight majority to merge, effective in 1898.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Kings County retained its status as one of New York State's counties, but the loss of Brooklyn's separate identity as a city was met with consternation by some residents at the time. Many newspapers of the day called the merger the "Great Mistake of 1898".[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/New_York_City_location_Brooklyn.svg/250px-New_York_City_location_Brooklyn.svg.png)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_location_Brooklyn.svg)

Location of Brooklyn (red) within New York City (remainder yellow)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/NY_Brooklyn_20190925_TM.jpg/250px-NY_Brooklyn_20190925_TM.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_Brooklyn_20190925_TM.jpg)

[USGS](/wiki/United_States_Geological_Survey "United States Geological Survey") map of Brooklyn (2019)

Brooklyn is 97 square miles (250 km2) in area, of which 71 square miles (180 km2) is land (73%), and 26 square miles (67 km2) is water (27%); the borough is the second-largest by land area among the New York City's boroughs. However, Kings County, coterminous with Brooklyn, is New York State's fourth-smallest [county](/wiki/County_(New_York) "County (New York)") by land area and third-smallest by total area.[[10]](#cite_note-GR1-10) Brooklyn lies at the southwestern end of Long Island, and the borough's western border constitutes the island's western tip.

Brooklyn's water borders are extensive and varied, including [Jamaica Bay](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay "Jamaica Bay"); the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"); [The Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows"), separating Brooklyn from the borough of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") in New York City and crossed by the [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge"); [Upper New York Bay](/wiki/Upper_New_York_Bay "Upper New York Bay"), separating Brooklyn from [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") and [Bayonne](/wiki/Bayonne,_New_Jersey "Bayonne, New Jersey") in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"); and the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), separating Brooklyn from the borough of Manhattan in New York City and traversed by the [Brooklyn-Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn-Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn-Battery Tunnel"), the [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"), and numerous routes of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"). To the east of Brooklyn lies the borough of Queens, which contains [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") in that borough's [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica,_Queens "Jamaica, Queens") neighborhood, approximately two miles from the border of Brooklyn's [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York,_Brooklyn "East New York, Brooklyn") neighborhood.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Climate")]

Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), Brooklyn experiences a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*),[[51]](#cite_note-Peel,_M._C.;_Finlayson,_B._L.;_McMahon,_T._A-51) with partial shielding from the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") and moderating influences from the Atlantic Ocean. Brooklyn receives plentiful precipitation all year round, with nearly 50 in (1,300 mm) yearly. The area averages 234 days with at least some sunshine annually, and averages 57% of possible sunshine annually, accumulating 2,535 hours of sunshine per annum.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Brooklyn lies in the [USDA](/wiki/USDA "USDA") [plant hardiness zone 7b](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

## Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Brooklyn's neighborhoods are dynamic in ethnic composition. For example, the early to mid-20th century, [Brownsville](/wiki/Brownsville,_Brooklyn "Brownsville, Brooklyn") had a majority of [Jewish](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City") residents; since the 1970s it has been majority African American. [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood,_Brooklyn "Midwood, Brooklyn") during the early 20th century was filled with ethnic [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American"), then filled with Jewish residents for nearly 50 years, and is slowly becoming a [Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistani_Americans "Pakistani Americans") enclave. Brooklyn's most populous racial group, white, declined from 97.2% in 1930 to 46.9% by 1990.[[59]](#cite_note-Gibson2005-59)

The borough attracts people previously living in other cities in the United States. Of these, most come from [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), and [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle").[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-US_Census_Bureau,_California,_racial_breakdown-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Community diversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Community diversity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Imatra_Society%27s_summer_festival.jpg/250px-Imatra_Society%27s_summer_festival.jpg)](/wiki/File:Imatra_Society%27s_summer_festival.jpg)

[Imatra Society](/wiki/Imatra_Society "Imatra Society"), consisting of [Finnish immigrants](/wiki/Finnish_Americans "Finnish Americans"), celebrating its summer festival in [Fort Hamilton, Brooklyn](/wiki/Fort_Hamilton "Fort Hamilton"), in 1894

Given New York City's role as a crossroads for immigration from around the world, Brooklyn has evolved a globally [cosmopolitan](/wiki/Cosmopolitanism "Cosmopolitanism") ambiance of its own, demonstrating a robust and growing demographic and cultural diversity with respect to metrics including nationality, religion, race, and [domiciliary partnership](/wiki/Domestic_partnership "Domestic partnership"). In 2010, 51.6% of the population was counted as members of religious congregations.[[67]](#cite_note-67) In 2014, there were 914 religious organizations in Brooklyn, the 10th most of all counties in the nation.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Brooklyn contains dozens of distinct neighborhoods representing many of the major culturally identified groups found within New York City. Among the most prominent are listed below:

#### Jewish American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Jewish American")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Hasidic_Family_in_Street_-_Borough_Park_-_Hasidic_District_-_Brooklyn.jpg/250px-Hasidic_Family_in_Street_-_Borough_Park_-_Hasidic_District_-_Brooklyn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hasidic_Family_in_Street_-_Borough_Park_-_Hasidic_District_-_Brooklyn.jpg)

The world's largest metropolitan [Hasidic Jewish](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism") community resides in Brooklyn.

Main article: [Jews in New York City](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City")

Over 600,000 [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews"), particularly [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") and [Hasidic Jews](/wiki/Hasidic_Judaism "Hasidic Judaism"), have become concentrated in such historically Jewish areas as [Borough Park](/wiki/Borough_Park,_Brooklyn "Borough Park, Brooklyn"), [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn"), and [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood,_Brooklyn "Midwood, Brooklyn"), where there are many [yeshivas](/wiki/Yeshiva "Yeshiva"), [synagogues](/wiki/Synagogue "Synagogue"), and [kosher](/wiki/Kosher "Kosher") restaurants, as well as a variety of Jewish businesses. Adjacent to Borough Park, the [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_Brooklyn "Kensington, Brooklyn") area housed a significant population of [Conservative Jews](/wiki/Conservative_Judaism "Conservative Judaism") (under the aegis of such nationally prominent midcentury rabbis as [Jacob Bosniak](/wiki/Jacob_Bosniak "Jacob Bosniak") and Abraham Heller)[[69]](#cite_note-69) when it was still considered to be a subsection of Flatbush; many of their defunct facilities have been repurposed to serve extensions of the Borough Park Hasidic community. Other notable religious Jewish neighborhoods with a longstanding cultural lineage include [Canarsie](/wiki/Canarsie,_Brooklyn "Canarsie, Brooklyn"), [Sea Gate](/wiki/Sea_Gate,_Brooklyn "Sea Gate, Brooklyn"), and [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), home to the [Chabad](/wiki/Chabad "Chabad") world headquarters. Neighborhoods with largely defunct yet historically notable Jewish populations include central Flatbush, East Flatbush, Brownsville, East New York, Bensonhurst and Sheepshead Bay (particularly its Madison subsection). Many hospitals in Brooklyn were started by Jewish charities, including [Maimonides Medical Center](/wiki/Maimonides_Medical_Center "Maimonides Medical Center") in Borough Park and Brookdale Hospital in East Flatbush.[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

According to the American Jewish Population Project in 2020, Brooklyn was home to over 480,000 Jews.[[72]](#cite_note-72) In 2023, the [UJA-Federation of New York](/wiki/UJA-Federation_of_New_York "UJA-Federation of New York") estimated that Brooklyn is home to 462,000 Jews, a large decrease compared to the 561,000 estimated in 2011.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The predominantly Jewish, Crown Heights (and later East Flatbush)-based Madison Democratic Club served as the borough's primary "clubhouse" political venue for decades until the ascendancy of [Meade Esposito](/wiki/Meade_Esposito "Meade Esposito")'s rival, Canarsie-based Thomas Jefferson Democratic Club in the 1960s and 1970s, playing an integral role in the rise of such figures as [Speaker of the New York State Assembly](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_New_York_State_Assembly "Speaker of the New York State Assembly") [Irwin Steingut](/wiki/Irwin_Steingut "Irwin Steingut"); his son, fellow Speaker [Stanley Steingut](/wiki/Stanley_Steingut "Stanley Steingut"); [New York City Mayor](/wiki/New_York_City_Mayor "New York City Mayor") [Abraham Beame](/wiki/Abraham_Beame "Abraham Beame"); real estate developer [Fred Trump](/wiki/Fred_Trump "Fred Trump"); Democratic district leader Beadie Markowitz; and political fixer Abraham "Bunny" Lindenbaum.

Many non-Orthodox Jews (ranging from observant members of various denominations to [atheists](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") of Jewish cultural heritage) are concentrated in [Ditmas Park](/wiki/Ditmas_Park,_Brooklyn "Ditmas Park, Brooklyn") and [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope,_Brooklyn "Park Slope, Brooklyn"), with smaller observant and culturally Jewish populations in Brooklyn Heights, Cobble Hill, Brighton Beach, and Coney Island.

#### Chinese American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Chinese American")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Brooklyn_Chinatown.png/250px-Brooklyn_Chinatown.png)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Chinatown.png)

[8th Avenue](/wiki/Eighth_Avenue_(Brooklyn)#Chinatown "Eighth Avenue (Brooklyn)") in Brooklyn's [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn") Chinatown

Main articles: [Chinatowns in Brooklyn](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_Brooklyn "Chinatowns in Brooklyn") and [Chinese people in New York City](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_New_York_City "Chinese people in New York City")

Over 200,000 [Chinese Americans](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_New_York_City "Chinese people in New York City") live throughout the southern parts of Brooklyn, primarily concentrated in [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn"), [Bensonhurst](/wiki/Bensonhurst,_Brooklyn "Bensonhurst, Brooklyn"), [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend,_Brooklyn "Gravesend, Brooklyn"), and [Homecrest](/wiki/Homecrest,_Brooklyn "Homecrest, Brooklyn"). Brooklyn is the borough that is home to the highest number of [Chinatowns in New York City](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_the_Americas#New_York_City "Chinatowns in the Americas"). The largest concentration is in Sunset Park along 8th Avenue, which has become known for its [Chinese culture](/wiki/Culture_of_China "Culture of China") since the opening of the now-defunct Winley Supermarket in 1986 spurred widespread settlement in the area. It is called ["Brooklyn's Chinatown"](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_Brooklyn "Chinatowns in Brooklyn") and originally it was a small Chinese enclave with [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") speakers being the main [Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") population during the late 1980s and 1990s, but since the 2000s, the Chinese population in the area dramatically shifted to majority [Fuzhounese Americans](/wiki/Fuzhounese_Americans "Fuzhounese Americans"), which contributed immensely to expanding this Chinatown, and bestowing the nicknames "[Fuzhou Town](/wiki/Chinatowns_in_Brooklyn#Fuzhou_Town,_Brooklyn "Chinatowns in Brooklyn") (福州埠), Brooklyn" or the "[Little Fuzhou](/wiki/Little_Fuzhou "Little Fuzhou") (小福州)" of Brooklyn. Many [Chinese restaurants](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine") can be found throughout Sunset Park, and the area hosts a popular [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") celebration. Since the 2000s going forward, the growing concentration of the Cantonese speaking population in Brooklyn have dramatically shifted to Bensonhurst/Gravesend and Homecrest creating newer Chinatowns of Brooklyn and these newer Brooklyn Chinatowns are known as "Brooklyn's Little Hong Kong/Guangdong" due to their Chinese populations being overwhelmingly Cantonese populated.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

#### African American and Caribbean American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: African American and Caribbean American")]

Main article: [Caribbean immigration to New York City](/wiki/Caribbean_immigration_to_New_York_City "Caribbean immigration to New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/West_Indian_Day_Parade_2008.jpg/250px-West_Indian_Day_Parade_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_Indian_Day_Parade_2008.jpg)

The [West Indian Day Parade](/wiki/West_Indian_Day_Parade "West Indian Day Parade") marching by the [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

Brooklyn's [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") and [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_people "Caribbean people") communities are spread throughout much of Brooklyn. Brooklyn's [West Indian](/wiki/West_Indian "West Indian") community is concentrated in the Crown Heights, Flatbush, [East Flatbush](/wiki/East_Flatbush,_Brooklyn "East Flatbush, Brooklyn"), Kensington, and Canarsie neighborhoods in central Brooklyn. Brooklyn is home to the largest community of West Indians outside of the Caribbean. Although the largest West Indian groups in Brooklyn are [Jamaicans](/wiki/Jamaican_American "Jamaican American"), [Guyanese](/wiki/Guyanese_Americans "Guyanese Americans") and [Haitians](/wiki/Haitian_Americans "Haitian Americans"), there are [West Indian immigrants](/wiki/West_Indian_Americans "West Indian Americans") from nearly every part of the Caribbean. Crown Heights and Flatbush are home to many of Brooklyn's West Indian restaurants and bakeries. Brooklyn has an annual, celebrated Carnival in the tradition of pre-Lenten celebrations in the islands.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Started by natives of [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidad_and_Tobago "Trinidad and Tobago"), the [West Indian Labor Day Parade](/wiki/West_Indian_Day_Parade "West Indian Day Parade") takes place every Labor Day on [Eastern Parkway](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway "Eastern Parkway"). The [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music") also holds the [DanceAfrica](/wiki/DanceAfrica "DanceAfrica") festival in late May, featuring street vendors and dance performances showcasing food and culture from all parts of Africa.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) Since the opening of the [IND Fulton Street Line](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line") in 1936, [Bedford-Stuyvesant](/wiki/Bedford-Stuyvesant,_Brooklyn "Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn") has been home to one of the most famous African American communities in the United States. Working-class communities remain prevalent in [Brownsville](/wiki/Brownsville,_Brooklyn "Brownsville, Brooklyn"), [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York,_Brooklyn "East New York, Brooklyn") and [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island,_Brooklyn "Coney Island, Brooklyn"), while remnants of similar communities in [Prospect Heights](/wiki/Prospect_Heights,_Brooklyn "Prospect Heights, Brooklyn"), [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn") and [Clinton Hill](/wiki/Clinton_Hill,_Brooklyn "Clinton Hill, Brooklyn") have endured amid widespread gentrification.

#### Hispanic American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Hispanic American")]

Further information: [Puerto Ricans in New York City](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans_in_New_York_City "Puerto Ricans in New York City") and [Nuyorican](/wiki/Nuyorican "Nuyorican")

In the aftermath of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and subsequent [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") initiatives that decimated longtime Manhattan enclaves (most notably on the [Upper West Side](/wiki/Upper_West_Side "Upper West Side")), Puerto Rican migrants began to settle in such waterfront industrial neighborhoods as [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn"), [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn") and [Gowanus](/wiki/Gowanus,_Brooklyn "Gowanus, Brooklyn"), near the shipyards and factories where they worked. The borough's Hispanic population diversified after the 1965 [Hart-Cellar Act](/wiki/Hart-Cellar_Act "Hart-Cellar Act") loosened restrictions on immigration from elsewhere in Latin America.

[Bushwick](/wiki/Bushwick,_Brooklyn "Bushwick, Brooklyn") has since emerged as the largest hub of Brooklyn's [Hispanic American](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") community. Like other Hispanic neighborhoods in New York City, Bushwick has an established [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Stateside_Puerto_Ricans "Stateside Puerto Ricans") presence, along with an influx of many [Dominicans](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans"), [South Americans](/wiki/South_America "South America"), [Central Americans](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and [Mexicans](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans"). As nearly 80% of Bushwick's population is Hispanic, its residents have created many businesses to support their various national and distinct traditions in food and other items. Sunset Park's population is 42% Hispanic, made up of these various ethnic groups. Brooklyn's main Hispanic groups are Puerto Ricans, Mexicans, Dominicans and [Ecuadorians](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Americans "Ecuadorian Americans"); they are spread out throughout the borough. Puerto Ricans and Dominicans are predominant in Bushwick, [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn")'s South Side and East New York. Mexicans (especially from the state of [Puebla](/wiki/Puebla "Puebla")) now predominate alongside Chinese immigrants in Sunset Park, although remnants of the neighborhood's once-substantial postwar Puerto Rican and Dominican communities continue to reside below 39th Street. Save for Red Hook (which remained roughly one-fifth Hispanic American as of the 2010 Census), the South Side and Sunset Park, similar postwar communities in other waterfront neighborhoods—including western Park Slope, the north end of Greenpoint,[[79]](#cite_note-79) and [Boerum Hill](/wiki/Boerum_Hill "Boerum Hill"), long considered the northern subsection of Gowanus—largely disappeared by the turn of the century due to various factors, including deindustrialization, ensuing gentrification and suburbanization among more affluent Dominicans and Puerto Ricans. A Panamanian enclave exists in [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn").

#### Russian and Ukrainian American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Russian and Ukrainian American")]

Main article: [Russian Americans in New York City](/wiki/Russian_Americans_in_New_York_City "Russian Americans in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Brighton_Beach_avenue.JPG/250px-Brighton_Beach_avenue.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brighton_Beach_avenue.JPG)

"Little Russia" or "Little [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa")" in [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach,_Brooklyn "Brighton Beach, Brooklyn")

Brooklyn is also home to many [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians") and [Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainians "Ukrainians"), who are mainly concentrated in the areas of [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach,_Brooklyn "Brighton Beach, Brooklyn") and [Sheepshead Bay](/wiki/Sheepshead_Bay,_Brooklyn "Sheepshead Bay, Brooklyn"). Brighton Beach features many Russian and Ukrainian businesses and has been nicknamed *[Little Russia](/wiki/Russian_Americans_in_New_York_City#Russiantownscape "Russian Americans in New York City")* and *Little [Odessa](/wiki/Odessa "Odessa")*, respectively. In the 1970s, [Soviet Jews won the right to immigrate](/wiki/Soviet_Jewry_Movement "Soviet Jewry Movement"), and many ended up in Brighton Beach. In recent years, the non-Jewish Russian and Ukrainian communities of Brighton Beach have grown, and the area is now home to a diverse collection of immigrants from across the [former USSR](/wiki/Post-Soviet_States "Post-Soviet States"). Smaller concentrations of Russian and Ukrainian Americans are scattered elsewhere in south Brooklyn, including Bay Ridge, Bensonhurst, Homecrest, Coney Island, and [Mill Basin](/wiki/Mill_Basin,_Brooklyn "Mill Basin, Brooklyn"). A growing community of [Uzbek Americans](/wiki/Uzbek_Americans "Uzbek Americans") have settled alongside them in recent years due to their ability to speak [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language").[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

#### Polish American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Polish American")]

Brooklyn's [Polish](/wiki/Polish-Americans "Polish-Americans") inhabitants are historically concentrated in [Greenpoint](/wiki/Greenpoint,_Brooklyn "Greenpoint, Brooklyn"), home to [Little Poland](/wiki/Little_Poland,_Brooklyn "Little Poland, Brooklyn"). Other longstanding settlements in [Borough Park](/wiki/Borough_Park,_Brooklyn "Borough Park, Brooklyn") and [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn") have endured, while more recent immigrants are scattered throughout the southern parts of Brooklyn alongside the Russian and Ukrainian American communities.

#### Italian American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Italian American")]

Main article: [Italians in New York City](/wiki/Italians_in_New_York_City "Italians in New York City")

Despite widespread migration to [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") and more suburban areas in metropolitan New York throughout the postwar era, notable concentrations of [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") continue to reside in the neighborhoods of [Bensonhurst](/wiki/Bensonhurst "Bensonhurst"), [Dyker Heights](/wiki/Dyker_Heights,_Brooklyn "Dyker Heights, Brooklyn"), [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn"), [Bath Beach](/wiki/Bath_Beach,_Brooklyn "Bath Beach, Brooklyn") and [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend,_Brooklyn "Gravesend, Brooklyn"). Less perceptible remnants of older communities have persisted in [Cobble Hill](/wiki/Cobble_Hill,_Brooklyn "Cobble Hill, Brooklyn") and [Carroll Gardens](/wiki/Carroll_Gardens,_Brooklyn "Carroll Gardens, Brooklyn"), where the homes of the remaining Italian Americans can often be contrasted with more recent [upper middle class](/wiki/Upper_middle_class "Upper middle class") residents through the display of small [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(art) "Madonna (art)") statues, the retention of plastic-metal stoop awnings and the use of [Formstone](/wiki/Formstone "Formstone") in house cladding. All of the aforementioned neighborhoods have retained Italian restaurants, bakeries, delicatessens, [pizzerias](/wiki/Pizzeria "Pizzeria"), cafes and social clubs.

#### Arab American & Muslim

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Arab American & Muslim")]

In the early 20th century, many [Lebanese](/wiki/Christianity_in_Lebanon "Christianity in Lebanon") and [Syrian Christians](/wiki/Christianity_in_Syria "Christianity in Syria") settled around [Atlantic Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue_(New_York_City) "Atlantic Avenue (New York City)") west of [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") in [Boerum Hill](/wiki/Boerum_Hill "Boerum Hill"); more recently, this area has evolved into a Yemeni commercial district.
More recent, predominantly [Muslim](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") [Arab immigrants](/wiki/Arab_Americans "Arab Americans"), especially [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") and [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_people "Lebanese people"), have moved into the southwest portion of Brooklyn, particularly to [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn"), where many Middle Eastern restaurants, hookah lounges, halal grocers, Islamic shops and mosques line the commercial thoroughfares of Fifth and Third Avenues below 86th Street. Brighton Beach is home to a growing [Pakistani American](/wiki/Pakistani_Americans "Pakistani Americans") community, while Midwood is home to *Little [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")* along [Coney Island Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island_Avenue "Coney Island Avenue") (recently co-named [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") Way). Pakistani Independence Day is celebrated every year with parades and parties on Coney Island Avenue. Just to the north, Kensington is one of New York's several emerging [Bangladeshi](/wiki/Bangladeshi_Americans "Bangladeshi Americans") enclaves.

#### Irish American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Irish American")]

Third-, fourth- and fifth-generation [Irish Americans](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") can be found throughout Brooklyn, with moderate concentrations[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] enduring in the neighborhoods of [Windsor Terrace](/wiki/Windsor_Terrace,_Brooklyn "Windsor Terrace, Brooklyn"), [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope,_Brooklyn "Park Slope, Brooklyn"), [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn"), [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park,_Brooklyn "Marine Park, Brooklyn") and [Gerritsen Beach](/wiki/Gerritsen_Beach,_Brooklyn "Gerritsen Beach, Brooklyn"). Historical communities also existed in [Vinegar Hill](/wiki/Vinegar_Hill,_Brooklyn "Vinegar Hill, Brooklyn") and other waterfront industrial neighborhoods, such as Greenpoint and Sunset Park. Paralleling the Italian American community, many moved to Staten Island and suburban areas in the postwar era. Those that stayed engendered close-knit, stable working-to-middle class communities through employment in the civil service (especially in law enforcement, transportation, and the [New York City Fire Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department")) and the building and construction trades, while others were subsumed by the [professional-managerial class](/wiki/Professional-managerial_class "Professional-managerial class") and largely shed the Irish American community's distinct cultural traditions (including continued worship in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and other social activities, such as [Irish stepdance](/wiki/Irish_stepdance "Irish stepdance") and frequenting Irish American bars).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### South Asian American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: South Asian American")]

While not as extensive as the [Indian American](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_area#New_York_City_boroughs "Indians in the New York City metropolitan area") population in [Queens](/wiki/Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America "Hindu Temple Society of North America"), younger professionals of [Asian Indian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans") origin are finding Brooklyn to be a convenient alternative to Manhattan to find housing. Nearly 30,000
Indian Americans call Brooklyn home.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Brighton Beach is home to a growing [Pakistani American](/wiki/Pakistani_Americans "Pakistani Americans") community, while Midwood is home to [Little Pakistan](/wiki/Little_Pakistan "Little Pakistan") along [Coney Island Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island_Avenue "Coney Island Avenue") recently renamed [Muhammad Ali Jinnah](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali_Jinnah "Muhammad Ali Jinnah") way. [Pakistan Independence Day](/wiki/Pakistan_Independence_Day "Pakistan Independence Day") is celebrated every year with parades and parties on Coney Island Avenue.
Just to the north, [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_Brooklyn "Kensington, Brooklyn") is one of New York's several emerging [Bangladeshi](/wiki/Bangladeshis "Bangladeshis") enclaves.

#### Greek American

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Greek American")]

Brooklyn's [Greek Americans](/wiki/Greek_Americans "Greek Americans") live throughout the borough. A historical concentration has endured in Bay Ridge and adjacent areas, where there is a noticeable cluster of Hellenic-focused schools, businesses and cultural institutions. Other businesses are situated in Downtown Brooklyn near Atlantic Avenue. As in much of the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), Greek-owned diners are found throughout the borough.

#### LGBTQ community

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: LGBTQ community")]

Main article: [LGBT culture in New York City § Brooklyn](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City#Brooklyn "LGBT culture in New York City")

Brooklyn is home to a large and growing number of same-sex couples. [Same-sex marriages in New York](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_York "Same-sex marriage in New York") were legalized on June 24, 2011, and were authorized to take place beginning 30 days thereafter.[[82]](#cite_note-82) The [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope,_Brooklyn "Park Slope, Brooklyn") neighborhood spearheaded the popularity of Brooklyn among lesbians, and [Prospect Heights](/wiki/Prospect_Heights,_Brooklyn "Prospect Heights, Brooklyn") has an LGBT residential presence.[[83]](#cite_note-RosenbergDunfordp379-83) Numerous neighborhoods have since become home to LGBT communities. Brooklyn Liberation March, the largest [transgender-rights](/wiki/Transgender_rights "Transgender rights") demonstration in LGBTQ history, took place on June 14, 2020, stretching from [Grand Army Plaza](/wiki/Grand_Army_Plaza "Grand Army Plaza") to [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn"), focused on supporting Black transgender lives, drawing an estimated 15,000 to 20,000 participants.[[84]](#cite_note-84)[[85]](#cite_note-85)

#### Artists-in-residence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Artists-in-residence")]

Brooklyn became a preferred site for artists and [hipsters](/wiki/Hipster_(contemporary_subculture) "Hipster (contemporary subculture)") to set up live/work spaces after being priced out of the same types of living arrangements in Manhattan. Various neighborhoods in Brooklyn, including Williamsburg, [DUMBO](/wiki/Dumbo,_Brooklyn "Dumbo, Brooklyn"), [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn"), and Park Slope evolved as popular neighborhoods for [artists-in-residence](/wiki/Artists-in-residence "Artists-in-residence"). However, rents and costs of living have since increased dramatically in these same neighborhoods, forcing artists to move to somewhat less expensive neighborhoods in Brooklyn or across Upper New York Bay to locales in New Jersey, such as [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") or [Hoboken](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey").[[86]](#cite_note-86)

At the 2020 census, 2,736,074 people lived in Brooklyn. The [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") had estimated Brooklyn's population increased by 2.2% to 2,559,903 between 2010 and 2019. Brooklyn's estimated population represented 30.7% of New York City's estimated population of 8,336,817; 33.5% of Long Island's population of 7,701,172; and 13.2% of New York State's population of 19,542,209.[[99]](#cite_note-:1-99) In 2020, the government of New York City projected Brooklyn's population at 2,648,403.[[100]](#cite_note-NYC_Open-100) The 2019 census estimates determined there were 958,567 households with an average of 2.66 persons per household.[[101]](#cite_note-:3-101) There were 1,065,399 housing units in 2019 and a median gross rent of $1,426. Citing growth, Brooklyn gained 9,696 building permits at the 2019 census estimates program.

The 2020 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimated the racial and ethnic makeup of Brooklyn was 35.4% [non-Hispanic white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites"), 26.7% [Black or African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 0.9% [American Indian or Alaska Native](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), 13.6% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 0.1% [Native Hawaiian and other Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"), 4.1% [two or more races](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"), and 18.9% [Hispanic or Latin American](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") of any race.[[97]](#cite_note-auto2-97) According to the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Brooklyn's population was (inclusive of "in combination with one or more other races") 43.3% White, including 35.4% non-Hispanic White; 31.9% Black, including 26.7% non-Hispanic black; 15.3% Asian; 1.6% Native American; 0.2% Pacific Islander; 8.7% "Two or More Races"; and 16.9% from other races. Hispanics and Latinos made up 18.9% of Brooklyn's population.[[105]](#cite_note-106) In 2010, Brooklyn had some neighborhoods segregated based on race, ethnicity, and religion. Overall, the southwest half of Brooklyn is racially mixed although it contains few black residents; the northeast section is mostly black and Hispanic/Latino.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Languages")]

Brooklyn has a high degree of [linguistic diversity](/wiki/Linguistic_diversity_index "Linguistic diversity index"). As of 2010, 54.1% (1,240,416) of Brooklyn residents ages 5 and older spoke [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") at home as a [primary language](/wiki/Primary_language "Primary language"), while 17.2% (393,340) spoke [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), 6.5% (148,012) [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), 5.3% (121,607) [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), 3.5% (79,469) [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language"), 2.8% (63,019) [French Creole](/wiki/French-based_creole_languages "French-based creole languages"), 1.4% (31,004) [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"), 1.2% (27,440) [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"), 1.0% (23,207) [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language"), 1.0% (22,763) [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), 1.0% (21,773) [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"), 0.9% (19,388) [various Indic languages](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages"), 0.7% (15,936) [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu_language "Urdu language"), and [African languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Africa "Languages of Africa") were spoken as a [main language](/wiki/Main_language "Main language") by 0.5% (12,305) of the population over the age of five. In total, 45.9% (1,051,456) of Brooklyn's population ages 5 and older spoke a [mother tongue](/wiki/Mother_tongue "Mother tongue") other than English.[[107]](#cite_note-108)
