Brooklyn has played a major role in various aspects of American culture, including literature, cinema, and theater. [Brooklyn's accent](/wiki/New_York_accent "New York accent") has often been portrayed as the "typical New Yorker accent" in American media, although this accent and its stereotypes are supposedly diminishing in currency.[[108]](#cite_note-109) Brooklyn's official colors are blue and gold.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### Cultural venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Cultural venues")]

Brooklyn hosts the world-renowned [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music"), the [Brooklyn Philharmonic](/wiki/Brooklyn_Philharmonic "Brooklyn Philharmonic"), and the second-largest public art collection in the United States, housed in the [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum").

The Brooklyn Museum, opened in 1897, is New York City's second-largest public art museum. It has in its permanent collection more than 1.5 million objects, from ancient Egyptian masterpieces to contemporary art. The [Brooklyn Children's Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Children%27s_Museum "Brooklyn Children's Museum"), the world's first museum dedicated to children, opened in December 1899. The only such New York State institution accredited by the [American Alliance of Museums](/wiki/American_Alliance_of_Museums "American Alliance of Museums"), it is one of the few globally to have a permanent collection – over 30,000 cultural objects and natural history specimens.

The Brooklyn Academy of Music (BAM) includes a 2,109-seat opera house, an 874-seat theater, and the art-house BAM Rose Cinemas. [Bargemusic](/wiki/Bargemusic "Bargemusic") and St. Ann's Warehouse are on the other side of Downtown Brooklyn in the [DUMBO](/wiki/DUMBO "DUMBO") arts district. [Brooklyn Technical High School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Technical_High_School "Brooklyn Technical High School") has the second-largest auditorium in New York City (after [Radio City Music Hall](/wiki/Radio_City_Music_Hall "Radio City Music Hall")), with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of over 3,000.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Media")]

#### Local periodicals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Local periodicals")]

Brooklyn has several local newspapers: The *[Brooklyn Daily Eagle](/wiki/Brooklyn_Eagle "Brooklyn Eagle")*, *Bay Currents* (Oceanfront Brooklyn), *Brooklyn View*, *[The Brooklyn Paper](/wiki/The_Brooklyn_Paper "The Brooklyn Paper")*, and Courier-Life Publications. Courier-Life Publications, owned by Rupert Murdoch's [News Corporation](/wiki/News_Corporation_(1980%E2%80%932013) "News Corporation (1980–2013)"), is Brooklyn's largest chain of newspapers. Brooklyn is also served by the major New York dailies, including *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, the *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")*, and the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")*. Several others are now defunct, including the *Brooklyn Union* (1867–1937),[[111]](#cite_note-nysl.nysed.gov/nysnp/all/424-112)[[112]](#cite_note-brooklyn-union/12381-113) and the *[Brooklyn Times](/wiki/Brooklyn_Times "Brooklyn Times")*.[[111]](#cite_note-nysl.nysed.gov/nysnp/all/424-112)

The borough is home to the arts and politics monthly *[Brooklyn Rail](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rail "Brooklyn Rail")*, as well as the arts and cultural quarterly *[Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_(magazine) "Cabinet (magazine)")*. *[Hello Mr.](/wiki/Hello_Mr. "Hello Mr.")* is also published in Brooklyn.

*Brooklyn Magazine* is one of the few glossy magazines about Brooklyn. Several others are now defunct, including *BKLYN Magazine* (a bimonthly lifestyle book owned by Joseph McCarthy, that saw itself as a vehicle for high-end advertisers in Manhattan and was mailed to 80,000 high-income households), *Brooklyn Bridge Magazine*, *The Brooklynite* (a free, glossy quarterly edited by Daniel Treiman), and *NRG* (edited by Gail Johnson and originally marketed as a local periodical for Clinton Hill and Fort Greene, but expanded in scope to become the self-proclaimed "Pulse of Brooklyn" and then the "Pulse of New York").[[113]](#cite_note-114)

#### Ethnic press

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Ethnic press")]

Brooklyn has a thriving ethnic press. *[El Diario La Prensa](/wiki/El_Diario_La_Prensa "El Diario La Prensa")*, the largest and oldest Spanish-language daily newspaper in the United States, maintains its corporate headquarters at 1 [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn").[[114]](#cite_note-115) Major ethnic publications include the Brooklyn–Queens Catholic paper *[The Tablet](/wiki/The_Tablet_(Diocese_of_Brooklyn) "The Tablet (Diocese of Brooklyn)")*, *[Hamodia](/wiki/Hamodia "Hamodia")*, an Orthodox Jewish daily, and *[The Jewish Press](/wiki/The_Jewish_Press "The Jewish Press")*, an Orthodox Jewish weekly. Many nationally distributed ethnic newspapers are based in Brooklyn. Over 60 ethnic groups, writing in 42 languages, publish some 300 non-English language magazines and newspapers in New York City. Among them is the quarterly *[L'Idea](/wiki/L%27Idea "L'Idea")*, a bilingual magazine printed in Italian and English since 1974. In addition, many newspapers published abroad, such as *[The Daily Gleaner](/wiki/Gleaner_Company "Gleaner Company")* and *[The Star](/wiki/The_Jamaica_Star "The Jamaica Star")* of Jamaica, are available in Brooklyn.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] *[Our Time Press](/wiki/Our_Time_Press "Our Time Press")*, published weekly by DBG Media, covers the Village of Brooklyn with a motto of "The Local Paper with the Global View".

#### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Television")]

The City of New York has an official television station, run by [NYC Media](/wiki/NYC_Media "NYC Media"), which features programming based in Brooklyn. [Brooklyn Community Access Television](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Community_Access_Television&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brooklyn Community Access Television (page does not exist)") is the borough's [public access channel](/wiki/Public_access_television "Public access television").[[115]](#cite_note-116) Its studios are at the [BRIC Arts Media](/wiki/BRIC_Arts_Media "BRIC Arts Media") venue, called BRIC House, located on [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Brooklyn) "Fulton Street (Brooklyn)") in the [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene "Fort Greene") section of the borough.[[116]](#cite_note-117)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Events")]

* The annual [Coney Island Mermaid Parade](/wiki/Coney_Island_Mermaid_Parade "Coney Island Mermaid Parade") (mid-to-late June) is a costume-and-float parade.[[117]](#cite_note-Ward,_Nathan-118)
* Coney Island also hosts the annual [Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest](/wiki/Nathan%27s_Hot_Dog_Eating_Contest "Nathan's Hot Dog Eating Contest") (July 4).[[117]](#cite_note-Ward,_Nathan-118)
* The annual [Labor Day Carnival](/wiki/Labor_Day_Carnival "Labor Day Carnival") (also known as the Labor Day Parade or West Indian Day Parade) takes place along [Eastern Parkway](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway "Eastern Parkway") in [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn").
* The Art of Brooklyn Film Festival runs annually around the second week of June.[[118]](#cite_note-119)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Economy")]

See also: [Economy of New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/The_Brooklyn_Tower_010.jpg/250px-The_Brooklyn_Tower_010.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Brooklyn_Tower_010.jpg)

[The Brooklyn Tower](/wiki/The_Brooklyn_Tower "The Brooklyn Tower"), the [tallest building in Brooklyn](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Brooklyn "List of tallest buildings in Brooklyn") and the [tallest in New York State outside Manhattan](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City "List of tallest buildings in New York City").

Brooklyn's job market is driven by three main factors: the performance of the national and city economy, population flows and the borough's position as a convenient back office for New York's businesses.[[119]](#cite_note-report-120)

Forty-four percent of Brooklyn's employed population, or 410,000 people, work in the borough; more than half of the borough's residents work outside its boundaries. As a result, economic conditions in Manhattan are important to the borough's jobseekers. Strong international immigration to Brooklyn generates jobs in services, retailing and construction.[[119]](#cite_note-report-120)

Since the late 20th century, Brooklyn has benefited from a steady influx of financial [back office](/wiki/Back_office "Back office") operations from Manhattan, the rapid growth of a [high-tech](/wiki/High-tech "High-tech") and entertainment economy in [DUMBO](/wiki/DUMBO,_Brooklyn "DUMBO, Brooklyn"), and strong growth in support services such as accounting, personal supply agencies, and computer services firms.[[119]](#cite_note-report-120)

Jobs in the borough were traditionally concentrated in manufacturing, but since 1975, Brooklyn has shifted from a manufacturing-based to a service-based economy. In 2004, 215,000 Brooklyn residents worked in the services sector, while 27,500 worked in manufacturing. Although manufacturing has declined, a substantial base has remained in apparel and niche manufacturing concerns such as furniture, fabricated metals, and food products.[[120]](#cite_note-121) The pharmaceutical company [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer") was founded in Brooklyn in 1869 and had a manufacturing plant in the borough for many years that employed thousands of workers, but the plant shut down in 2008. However, new light-manufacturing concerns in packaging organic and high-end food have sprung up in the old plant.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Established as a [shipbuilding](/wiki/Shipbuilding "Shipbuilding") facility in 1801, the [Brooklyn Navy Yard](/wiki/Brooklyn_Navy_Yard "Brooklyn Navy Yard") employed 70,000 people at its peak during World War II and was then the largest employer in the borough. The *[Missouri](/wiki/USS_Missouri_(BB-63) "USS Missouri (BB-63)")*, the ship on which the Japanese formally surrendered, was built there, as was the *[Maine](/wiki/USS_Maine_(ACR-1) "USS Maine (ACR-1)")*, whose sinking off Havana led to the start of the Spanish–American War. The iron-sided Civil War vessel the *[Monitor](/wiki/USS_Monitor "USS Monitor")* was built in Greenpoint. From 1968 to 1979 [Seatrain Shipbuilding](/wiki/Seatrain_Lines "Seatrain Lines") was the major employer.[[122]](#cite_note-123) Later tenants include industrial design firms, food processing businesses, artisans, and the film and television production industry. About 230 private-sector firms providing 4,000 jobs are at the Yard.

Construction and services are the fastest-growing sectors.[[123]](#cite_note-124) Most employers in Brooklyn are small businesses. In 2000, 91% of the approximately 38,704 business establishments in Brooklyn had fewer than 20 employees.[[124]](#cite_note-125) As of August 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit), the borough's unemployment rate was 5.9%.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

Brooklyn is also home to many banks and [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union"). According to the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation"), there were 37 banks and 26 credit unions operating in the borough in 2010.[[126]](#cite_note-127)[[127]](#cite_note-128)

The [rezoning](/wiki/Zoning_in_the_United_States "Zoning in the United States") of [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") has generated over US$10 billion of private investment and $300 million in public improvements since 2004. Brooklyn is also attracting numerous [high technology](/wiki/High_technology "High technology") [start-up companies](/wiki/Start-up_company "Start-up company"), as [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley"), the [metonym](/wiki/Metonym "Metonym") for New York City's [entrepreneurship ecosystem](/wiki/Entrepreneurship_ecosystem "Entrepreneurship ecosystem"), has expanded from [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") into Brooklyn.[[128]](#cite_note-129)

## Parks and other attractions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Parks and other attractions")]

See also: [Tourism in New York City](/wiki/Tourism_in_New_York_City "Tourism in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ad/Bbg_cherry_esplande.jpg/330px-Bbg_cherry_esplande.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bbg_cherry_esplande.jpg)

[Kwanzan Cherries](/wiki/Prunus_serrulata "Prunus serrulata") in bloom at Brooklyn Botanic Garden

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Coney_Island_Beach-1.jpg/330px-Coney_Island_Beach-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coney_Island_Beach-1.jpg)

[Astroland](/wiki/Astroland "Astroland") in [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island")

* [Brooklyn Botanic Garden](/wiki/Brooklyn_Botanic_Garden "Brooklyn Botanic Garden"): adjacent to Prospect Park is the 52-acre (21 ha) botanical garden, which includes a cherry tree esplanade, a one-acre (0.4 ha) rose garden, a Japanese hill, and pond garden, a fragrance garden, a water lily pond esplanade, several conservatories, a rock garden, a native flora garden, a *[bonsai](/wiki/Bonsai "Bonsai")* tree collection, and children's gardens and discovery exhibits.
* [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island") developed as a playground for the rich in the early 1900s, but it grew as one of America's first amusement grounds and attracted crowds from all over New York. The [Cyclone rollercoaster](/wiki/Coney_Island_Cyclone "Coney Island Cyclone"), built-in 1927, is on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places"). The 1920 Wonder Wheel and other rides are still operational. Coney Island went into decline in the 1970s but has undergone a renaissance.[[129]](#cite_note-130)
* [Floyd Bennett Field](/wiki/Floyd_Bennett_Field "Floyd Bennett Field"): the first municipal airport in New York City and long-closed for operations, is now part of the [National Park System](/wiki/National_Park_System "National Park System"). Many of the historic hangars and runways are still extant. Nature trails and diverse habitats are found within the park, including [salt marsh](/wiki/Salt_marsh "Salt marsh") and a restored area of [shortgrass prairie](/wiki/Shortgrass_prairie "Shortgrass prairie") that was once widespread on the [Hempstead Plains](/wiki/Hempstead_Plains "Hempstead Plains").
* [Green-Wood Cemetery](/wiki/Green-Wood_Cemetery "Green-Wood Cemetery"), founded by the social reformer Henry Evelyn Pierrepont in 1838,[[130]](#cite_note-131) is an early [rural cemetery](/wiki/Rural_cemetery "Rural cemetery"). It is the burial ground of many notable New Yorkers.
* [Jamaica Bay Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay_Wildlife_Refuge "Jamaica Bay Wildlife Refuge"): a unique Federal wildlife refuge straddling the Brooklyn–Queens border, part of [Gateway National Recreation Area](/wiki/Gateway_National_Recreation_Area "Gateway National Recreation Area")
* [New York Transit Museum](/wiki/New_York_Transit_Museum "New York Transit Museum") displays historical artifacts of Greater New York's subway, commuter rail, and bus systems; it is at Court Street, a former [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") station in [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights "Brooklyn Heights") on the [Fulton Street Line](/wiki/IND_Fulton_Street_Line "IND Fulton Street Line").
* [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)") is a public park in central Brooklyn encompassing 585 acres (2.37 km2).[[131]](#cite_note-parkstat-132) The park was designed by [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted") and [Calvert Vaux](/wiki/Calvert_Vaux "Calvert Vaux"), who created Manhattan's [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"). Attractions include the Long Meadow, a 90-acre (36 ha) meadow, the Picnic House, which houses offices and a hall that can accommodate parties with up to 175 guests; [Litchfield Villa](/wiki/Litchfield_Villa "Litchfield Villa"), [Prospect Park Zoo](/wiki/Prospect_Park_Zoo "Prospect Park Zoo"), the [Boathouse](/wiki/Boathouse_on_the_Lullwater_of_the_Lake_in_Prospect_Park "Boathouse on the Lullwater of the Lake in Prospect Park"), housing a visitors center and the first urban [Audubon Center](/wiki/National_Audubon_Society "National Audubon Society");[[132]](#cite_note-auduboncenter-133) Brooklyn's only lake, covering 60 acres (24 ha); the Prospect Park Bandshell that hosts free outdoor concerts in the summertime; and various sports and fitness activities including seven baseball fields. Prospect Park hosts a popular annual Halloween Parade.
* [Fort Greene Park](/wiki/Fort_Greene_Park "Fort Greene Park") is a public park in the [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn") Neighborhood. The park contains the [Prison Ship Martyrs' Monument](/wiki/Fort_Greene_Park#Prison_Ship_Martyrs'_Monument "Fort Greene Park"), a monument to American prisoners during the Revolutionary War.

Further information: [Wildlife of Brooklyn](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Brooklyn "Wildlife of Brooklyn")

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sports")]

Main article: [Sports in Brooklyn](/wiki/Sports_in_Brooklyn "Sports in Brooklyn")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Barclays_Center_Rain_Night.jpg/330px-Barclays_Center_Rain_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barclays_Center_Rain_Night.jpg)

The Barclays Center in [Pacific Park](/wiki/Pacific_Park,_Brooklyn "Pacific Park, Brooklyn") within [Prospect Heights](/wiki/Prospect_Heights,_Brooklyn "Prospect Heights, Brooklyn"), home of the [Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") and [Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty")

Brooklyn's major professional sports team is the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")'s [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets"). The Nets moved into the borough in 2012, and play their home games at [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center") in Prospect Heights. Previously, the Nets had played in [Uniondale, New York](/wiki/Uniondale,_New_York "Uniondale, New York") and in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey").[[133]](#cite_note-134) In April 2020, the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") of the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA") were sold to the Nets' owners and moved their home venue from [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") to the Barclays Center.

Barclays Center was also the home arena for the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")'s [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders") full-time from 2015 to 2018, then part-time from 2018 to 2020 (alternating with [Nassau Coliseum](/wiki/Nassau_Veterans_Memorial_Coliseum "Nassau Veterans Memorial Coliseum") in Uniondale). The Islanders had originally played at Nassau Coliseum full-time since their inception until 2015 when their lease at the venue expired and the team moved to Barclays Center. In 2020, the team returned to Nassau Coliseum full-time for one season before moving to the [UBS Arena](/wiki/UBS_Arena "UBS Arena") in Elmont, New York in 2021.

Brooklyn also has a storied sports history. It has been home to many famous sports figures such as [Joe Paterno](/wiki/Joe_Paterno "Joe Paterno"), [Vince Lombardi](/wiki/Vince_Lombardi "Vince Lombardi"), [Mike Tyson](/wiki/Mike_Tyson "Mike Tyson"), [Zab Judah](/wiki/Zab_Judah "Zab Judah"), [Joe Torre](/wiki/Joe_Torre "Joe Torre"), [Sandy Koufax](/wiki/Sandy_Koufax "Sandy Koufax"), [Billy Cunningham](/wiki/Billy_Cunningham "Billy Cunningham") and [Vitas Gerulaitis](/wiki/Vitas_Gerulaitis "Vitas Gerulaitis"). Basketball legend [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan") was born in Brooklyn though he grew up in [Wilmington, North Carolina](/wiki/Wilmington,_North_Carolina "Wilmington, North Carolina").

In the earliest days of organized baseball, Brooklyn teams dominated the new game. The second recorded game of baseball was played near what is now [Fort Greene Park](/wiki/Fort_Greene_Park "Fort Greene Park") on October 24, 1845. Brooklyn's [Excelsiors](/wiki/Excelsior_of_Brooklyn "Excelsior of Brooklyn"), [Atlantics](/wiki/Brooklyn_Atlantics "Brooklyn Atlantics") and [Eckfords](/wiki/Eckford_of_Brooklyn "Eckford of Brooklyn") were the leading teams from the mid-1850s through the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), and there were dozens of local teams with neighborhood league play, such as at [Mapleton Oval](/wiki/Mapleton,_Brooklyn "Mapleton, Brooklyn").[[134]](#cite_note-BrooklynBallParks.com-135) During this "Brooklyn era", baseball evolved into the modern game: the first [fastball](/wiki/Fastball "Fastball"), first [changeup](/wiki/Changeup "Changeup"), first [batting average](/wiki/Batting_average_(baseball) "Batting average (baseball)"), first [triple play](/wiki/Triple_play "Triple play"), [first pro baseball player](/wiki/Jim_Creighton "Jim Creighton"), first [enclosed ballpark](/wiki/Union_Grounds "Union Grounds"), first [scorecard](/wiki/Baseball_scorekeeping "Baseball scorekeeping"), first known African-American team, first black championship game, first road trip, first gambling scandal, and first eight pennant winners were all in or from Brooklyn.[[135]](#cite_note-136)

Brooklyn's most famous historical team, the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers"), named for "trolley dodgers" played at [Ebbets Field](/wiki/Ebbets_Field "Ebbets Field").[[136]](#cite_note-137) In 1947 [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") was hired by the Dodgers as the first African-American player in Major League Baseball in the modern era. In 1955, the Dodgers, perennial National League pennant winners, won the only [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") for Brooklyn against their rival [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"). The event was marked by mass euphoria and celebrations. Just two years later, the Dodgers moved to Los Angeles. [Walter O'Malley](/wiki/Walter_O%27Malley "Walter O'Malley"), the team's owner at the time, is still vilified, even by Brooklynites too young to remember the Dodgers as Brooklyn's ball club.

After a 43-year hiatus, professional baseball returned to the borough in 2001 with the [Brooklyn Cyclones](/wiki/Brooklyn_Cyclones "Brooklyn Cyclones"), a [minor league](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") team that plays in [MCU Park](/wiki/MCU_Park "MCU Park") in [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"). They are an affiliate of the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets").

The minor-league [New York Cosmos](/wiki/New_York_Cosmos_(2013%E2%80%932020) "New York Cosmos (2013–2020)") soccer club played its home games at MCU Park in 2017.[[137]](#cite_note-138) A new [Brooklyn FC](/wiki/Brooklyn_FC_(USL) "Brooklyn FC (USL)") will begin play in 2024, fielding a women's team in the first-division [USL Super League](/wiki/USL_Super_League "USL Super League") and a men's team in the second-division [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship") beginning in 2025.[[138]](#cite_note-139)[[139]](#cite_note-140)

Brooklyn once had a [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") team named the [Brooklyn Lions](/wiki/Brooklyn_Lions "Brooklyn Lions") in 1926, who played at Ebbets Field.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

In [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union"), [Rugby United New York](/wiki/Rugby_United_New_York "Rugby United New York") joined [Major League Rugby](/wiki/Major_League_Rugby "Major League Rugby") in 2019 and played their home games at MCU Park through the 2021 season.

Brooklyn has one of the most active recreational fishing fleets in the United States. In addition to a large private fleet along Jamaica Bay, there is a substantial public fleet within Sheepshead Bay. Species caught include Black Fish, Porgy, Striped Bass, Black Sea Bass, Fluke, and Flounder.[[141]](#cite_note-142)[[142]](#cite_note-143)[[143]](#cite_note-144)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

See also: [Government and politics in Brooklyn](/wiki/Government_and_politics_in_Brooklyn "Government and politics in Brooklyn")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Bk_Boro_Hall_summer_dusk_jeh.JPG/250px-Bk_Boro_Hall_summer_dusk_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bk_Boro_Hall_summer_dusk_jeh.JPG)

upright=1.1[Brooklyn Borough Hall](/wiki/Brooklyn_Borough_Hall "Brooklyn Borough Hall")

Each of New York City's five counties, coterminous with each [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)"), has its own criminal court system and [District Attorney](/wiki/District_Attorney "District Attorney"), the chief public prosecutor who is directly elected by popular vote. Brooklyn has 16 City Council members, the largest number of any of the five boroughs. The Brooklyn Borough Government includes a [borough president](/wiki/Borough_president "Borough president") and a court, library, borough government board, head of borough government, deputy head of borough government and deputy borough government president.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Antonio_Reynoso.jpg/250px-Antonio_Reynoso.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antonio_Reynoso.jpg)

Brooklyn [borough president](/wiki/Borough_president "Borough president") [Antonio Reynoso](/wiki/Antonio_Reynoso "Antonio Reynoso")

Brooklyn has 18 of the city's 59 community districts, each served by an unpaid [community board](/wiki/Community_boards_in_New_York_City "Community boards in New York City") with advisory powers under the city's [Uniform Land Use Review Procedure](/wiki/Uniform_Land_Use_Review_Procedure "Uniform Land Use Review Procedure"). Each board has a paid district manager who acts as an interlocutor with city agencies. The [Kings County Democratic County Committee](/wiki/Kings_County_Democratic_County_Committee "Kings County Democratic County Committee") (aka the Brooklyn Democratic Party) is the county committee of the Democratic Party in Brooklyn.

The [United States Postal Service](/wiki/United_States_Postal_Service "United States Postal Service") operates post offices in Brooklyn. The [Brooklyn Main Post Office](/wiki/Federal_Building_and_Post_Office_(Brooklyn) "Federal Building and Post Office (Brooklyn)") is located at 271 [Cadman Plaza](/wiki/Cadman_Plaza "Cadman Plaza") East in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn").[[144]](#cite_note-145)

| **[1976](/wiki/1976_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1976 United States presidential election in New York")** | 190,728 | 31.08% | 419,382 | **68.34%** | 3,533 | 0.58% |
| **[1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1980 United States presidential election in New York")** | 200,306 | 38.44% | 288,893 | **55.44%** | 31,893 | 6.12% |
| **[1984](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1984 United States presidential election in New York")** | 230,064 | 38.29% | 368,518 | **61.34%** | 2,189 | 0.36% |
| **[1988](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1988 United States presidential election in New York")** | 178,961 | 32.60% | 363,916 | **66.28%** | 6,142 | 1.12% |
| **[1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1992 United States presidential election in New York")** | 133,344 | 22.93% | 411,183 | **70.70%** | 37,067 | 6.37% |
| **[1996](/wiki/1996_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1996 United States presidential election in New York")** | 81,406 | 15.08% | 432,232 | **80.07%** | 26,195 | 4.85% |
| **[2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2000 United States presidential election in New York")** | 96,609 | 15.65% | 497,513 | **80.60%** | 23,115 | 3.74% |
| **[2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2004 United States presidential election in New York")** | 167,149 | 24.30% | 514,973 | **74.86%** | 5,762 | 0.84% |
| **[2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2008 United States presidential election in New York")** | 151,872 | 19.99% | 603,525 | **79.43%** | 4,451 | 0.59% |
| **[2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2012 United States presidential election in New York")** | 124,551 | 16.90% | 604,443 | **82.02%** | 7,988 | 1.08% |
| **[2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2016 United States presidential election in New York")** | 141,044 | 17.51% | 640,553 | **79.51%** | 24,008 | 2.98% |
| **[2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2020 United States presidential election in New York")** | 202,772 | 22.14% | 703,310 | **76.78%** | 9,927 | 1.08% |
| **[2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2024 United States presidential election in New York")** | 233,964 | 27.40% | 601,265 | **70.43%** | 18,515 | 2.17% |

As is the case with sister boroughs Manhattan and the Bronx, Brooklyn has not voted for a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") in a national [presidential election](/wiki/United_States_presidential_election "United States presidential election") since [Calvin Coolidge](/wiki/Calvin_Coolidge "Calvin Coolidge") in [1924](/wiki/1924_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1924 United States presidential election in New York"). In the [2008 presidential election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2008 United States presidential election in New York"), Democrat [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") received 79.4% of the vote in Brooklyn while Republican [John McCain](/wiki/John_McCain "John McCain") received 20.0%. In [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "2012 United States presidential election in New York"), Barack Obama increased his Democratic margin of victory in the borough, dominating Brooklyn with 82.0% of the vote to Republican [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney")'s 16.9%.[[145]](#cite_note-DaveLeip-146)

In 2024, Republican [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") reached 27% of the vote, and held [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") at just over 70%, a significant shift from [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden")'s performance of over 76% in 2020. While still a decisive Democratic victory, this was the strongest Republican support in Brooklyn since 1988, and the largest number of raw Republican votes there since 1972.[[145]](#cite_note-DaveLeip-146)

### Federal representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Federal representation")]

As of 2023, four Democrats and one Republican represented Brooklyn in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"). One congressional district lies entirely within the borough.[[148]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-149)

* [Nydia Velázquez](/wiki/Nydia_Vel%C3%A1zquez "Nydia Velázquez") (first elected in 1992) represents [New York's 7th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_7th_congressional_district "New York's 7th congressional district"), which includes the central-west Brooklyn neighborhoods of [Boerum Hill](/wiki/Boerum_Hill "Boerum Hill"), [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights "Brooklyn Heights"), [Bushwick](/wiki/Bushwick,_Brooklyn "Bushwick, Brooklyn"), [Carroll Gardens](/wiki/Carroll_Gardens,_Brooklyn "Carroll Gardens, Brooklyn"), [Cobble Hill](/wiki/Cobble_Hill,_Brooklyn "Cobble Hill, Brooklyn"), [Dumbo](/wiki/Dumbo,_Brooklyn "Dumbo, Brooklyn"), [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York,_Brooklyn "East New York, Brooklyn"), [East Williamsburg](/wiki/East_Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "East Williamsburg, Brooklyn"), [Greenpoint](/wiki/Greenpoint,_Brooklyn "Greenpoint, Brooklyn"), [Gowanus](/wiki/Gowanus,_Brooklyn "Gowanus, Brooklyn"), [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn"), [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn"), and [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn"). The district also covers a small portion of [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens").[[148]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-149)
* [Hakeem Jeffries](/wiki/Hakeem_Jeffries "Hakeem Jeffries") (first elected in 2012) represents [New York's 8th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_8th_congressional_district "New York's 8th congressional district"), which includes the southern Brooklyn neighborhoods of [Bedford-Stuyvesant](/wiki/Bedford-Stuyvesant,_Brooklyn "Bedford-Stuyvesant, Brooklyn"), [Bergen Beach](/wiki/Bergen_Beach,_Brooklyn "Bergen Beach, Brooklyn"), [Brighton Beach](/wiki/Brighton_Beach "Brighton Beach"), [Brownsville](/wiki/Brownsville,_Brooklyn "Brownsville, Brooklyn"), [Canarsie](/wiki/Canarsie,_Brooklyn "Canarsie, Brooklyn"), [Clinton Hill](/wiki/Clinton_Hill,_Brooklyn "Clinton Hill, Brooklyn"), [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), [East Flatbush](/wiki/East_Flatbush,_Brooklyn "East Flatbush, Brooklyn"), [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York,_Brooklyn "East New York, Brooklyn"), [Fort Greene](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn"), [Gerritsen Beach](/wiki/Gerritsen_Beach,_Brooklyn "Gerritsen Beach, Brooklyn"), [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park,_Brooklyn "Marine Park, Brooklyn"), [Mill Basin](/wiki/Mill_Basin,_Brooklyn "Mill Basin, Brooklyn"), [Ocean Hill](/wiki/Ocean_Hill,_Brooklyn "Ocean Hill, Brooklyn"), [Sheepshead Bay](/wiki/Sheepshead_Bay,_Brooklyn "Sheepshead Bay, Brooklyn"), and [Spring Creek](/wiki/Spring_Creek,_Brooklyn "Spring Creek, Brooklyn"). The district also covers a small portion of Queens.[[148]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-149)
* [Yvette Clarke](/wiki/Yvette_Clarke "Yvette Clarke") (first elected in 2006) represents [New York's 9th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_9th_congressional_district "New York's 9th congressional district"), which includes the central and southern Brooklyn neighborhoods of [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), [East Flatbush](/wiki/East_Flatbush,_Brooklyn "East Flatbush, Brooklyn"), [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush,_Brooklyn "Flatbush, Brooklyn"), [Midwood](/wiki/Midwood,_Brooklyn "Midwood, Brooklyn"), [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope "Park Slope"), [Prospect Heights](/wiki/Prospect_Heights,_Brooklyn "Prospect Heights, Brooklyn"), [Prospect Lefferts Gardens](/wiki/Prospect_Lefferts_Gardens "Prospect Lefferts Gardens"), and [Windsor Terrace](/wiki/Windsor_Terrace,_Brooklyn "Windsor Terrace, Brooklyn").[[148]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-149)
* [Dan Goldman](/wiki/Dan_Goldman "Dan Goldman") (first elected in 2022) represents [New York's 10th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_10th_congressional_district "New York's 10th congressional district"), which includes the southwestern Brooklyn neighborhoods of Midwood, Red Hook, Sunset Park, [Bensonhurst](/wiki/Bensonhurst,_Brooklyn "Bensonhurst, Brooklyn"), [Borough Park](/wiki/Borough_Park,_Brooklyn "Borough Park, Brooklyn"), [Gravesend](/wiki/Gravesend,_Brooklyn "Gravesend, Brooklyn"), [Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_Brooklyn "Kensington, Brooklyn"), and [Mapleton](/wiki/Mapleton,_Brooklyn "Mapleton, Brooklyn"). The district also covers the [West Side](/wiki/West_Side_(Manhattan) "West Side (Manhattan)") of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").[[148]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-149)
* [Nicole Malliotakis](/wiki/Nicole_Malliotakis "Nicole Malliotakis") (first elected in 2020) represents [New York's 11th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_11th_congressional_district "New York's 11th congressional district"), which includes the southwestern Brooklyn neighborhoods of Bensonhurst, Gravesend, [Bath Beach](/wiki/Bath_Beach,_Brooklyn "Bath Beach, Brooklyn"), [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge,_Brooklyn "Bay Ridge, Brooklyn"), and [Dyker Heights](/wiki/Dyker_Heights,_Brooklyn "Dyker Heights, Brooklyn"). The district also covers all of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island").[[148]](#cite_note-govtrack.us_2018-149)

## Housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Housing")]

Brooklyn offers a wide array of private housing, as well as public housing, which is administered by the [New York City Housing Authority](/wiki/New_York_City_Housing_Authority "New York City Housing Authority") (NYCHA). Affordable rental and co-operative housing units throughout the borough were created under the [Mitchell–Lama Housing Program](/wiki/Mitchell%E2%80%93Lama_Housing_Program "Mitchell–Lama Housing Program").[[149]](#cite_note-150)

There were 1,101,441 housing units in 2022[[4]](#cite_note-BrooklynQuickFacts-4) at an average density of 15,876 units per square mile (6,130/km2). Public housing administered by NYCHA accounts for more than 100,000 residents in nearly 50,000 units in 2023.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Education")]

See also: [Education in New York City](/wiki/Education_in_New_York_City "Education in New York City") and [List of high schools in New York City](/wiki/List_of_high_schools_in_New_York_City "List of high schools in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Bklyn_Tech_HS_from_Ashland_jeh.jpg/250px-Bklyn_Tech_HS_from_Ashland_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bklyn_Tech_HS_from_Ashland_jeh.jpg)

Brooklyn Tech as seen from Ashland Place in Fort Greene

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/2016_Brooklyn_College_Library.jpg/250px-2016_Brooklyn_College_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:2016_Brooklyn_College_Library.jpg)

The Brooklyn College library, part of the original campus laid out by Randolph Evans, now known as "East Quad"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Brooklyn_Law_School_Fell_Hall.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Law_School_Fell_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Law_School_Fell_Hall.jpg)

[Brooklyn Law School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Law_School "Brooklyn Law School")'s 1994 [new classical](/wiki/New_Classical_Architecture "New Classical Architecture") "Fell Hall" tower, by architect [Robert A. M. Stern](/wiki/Robert_A._M._Stern "Robert A. M. Stern")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Wunsch_building_among_MetroTech.jpg/250px-Wunsch_building_among_MetroTech.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wunsch_building_among_MetroTech.jpg)

[NYU Tandon](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering") Wunsch Building

Education in Brooklyn is provided by a vast number of public and private institutions. Non-charter public schools in the borough are managed by the [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education"),[[151]](#cite_note-152) the largest public school system in the United States.

[Brooklyn Technical High School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Technical_High_School "Brooklyn Technical High School"), commonly called Brooklyn Tech, a New York City public high school, is the largest specialized high school for science, mathematics, and technology in the United States.[[152]](#cite_note-153) Brooklyn Tech opened in 1922. Brooklyn Tech is across the street from [Fort Greene Park](/wiki/Fort_Greene_Park "Fort Greene Park"). This high school was built from 1930 to 1933 at a cost of about $6 million and is 12 stories high. It covers about half of a city block.[[153]](#cite_note-154) Brooklyn Tech is noted for its famous alumni[[154]](#cite_note-155) (including two Nobel Laureates), its academics, and a large number of graduates attending prestigious universities.

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Higher education")]

#### Public colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Public colleges")]

[Brooklyn College](/wiki/Brooklyn_College "Brooklyn College") is a senior college of the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") (CUNY), and was the first public coeducational [liberal arts college](/wiki/Liberal_arts_college "Liberal arts college") in New York City. The college ranked in the top 10 nationally for the second consecutive year in [Princeton Review](/wiki/Princeton_Review "Princeton Review")'s 2006 guidebook, *America's Best Value Colleges*. Many of its students are first and second-generation Americans. Founded in 1970, [Medgar Evers College](/wiki/Medgar_Evers_College "Medgar Evers College") is a senior college of the City University of New York. The college offers programs at the baccalaureate and associate degree levels, as well as adult and continuing education classes for central Brooklyn residents, corporations, government agencies, and community organizations. Medgar Evers College is a few blocks east of [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)") in [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn").

CUNY's [New York City College of Technology](/wiki/New_York_City_College_of_Technology "New York City College of Technology") (City Tech) of The City University of New York (Downtown Brooklyn/Brooklyn Heights) is the largest public college of technology in New York State and a national model for technological education. Established in 1946, City Tech can trace its roots to 1881 when the Technical Schools of the Metropolitan Museum of Art were renamed the New York Trade School. That institution—which became the Voorhees Technical Institute many decades later—was soon a model for the development of technical and vocational schools worldwide. In 1971, Voorhees was incorporated into City Tech.

[SUNY Downstate College of Medicine](/wiki/SUNY_Downstate_College_of_Medicine "SUNY Downstate College of Medicine"), founded as the Long Island College Hospital in 1860, is the oldest hospital-based medical school in the United States. The Medical Center comprises the College of Medicine, College of Health Related Professions, College of Nursing, School of Public Health, School of Graduate Studies, and University Hospital of Brooklyn. The Nobel Prize winner [Robert F. Furchgott](/wiki/Robert_F._Furchgott "Robert F. Furchgott") was a member of its faculty. Half of the Medical Center's students are minorities or immigrants. The College of Medicine has the highest percentage of minority students of any medical school in New York State.

#### Private colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Private colleges")]

[Adelphi University](/wiki/Adelphi_University "Adelphi University"), based in [Garden City](/wiki/Garden_City,_New_York "Garden City, New York"), moved its Manhattan Campus in 2023 to a new location on Livingston Street in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"). The move marks a return to Brooklyn for the university, which originated on Adelphi Street with the Adelphi Academy. The facility is shared with [St. Francis College](/wiki/St._Francis_College "St. Francis College"), which has created a new campus at 179 Livingston Street.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

[Brooklyn Law School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Law_School "Brooklyn Law School") was founded in 1901 and is notable for its diverse student body. Women and African Americans were enrolled in 1909. According to the Leiter Report, a compendium of law school rankings published by [Brian Leiter](/wiki/Brian_Leiter "Brian Leiter"), Brooklyn Law School places 31st nationally for the quality of students.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

[Long Island University](/wiki/Long_Island_University "Long Island University") is a private university headquartered in [Brookville](/wiki/Brookville,_New_York "Brookville, New York") on [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), with a campus in Downtown Brooklyn with 6,417 undergraduate students. The Brooklyn campus has strong science and medical technology programs, at the graduate and undergraduate levels.

[Pratt Institute](/wiki/Pratt_Institute "Pratt Institute"), in [Clinton Hill](/wiki/Clinton_Hill,_Brooklyn "Clinton Hill, Brooklyn"), is a private college founded in 1887 with programs in engineering, architecture, and the arts. Some buildings in the school's Brooklyn campus are [official landmarks](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission"). Pratt has over 4700 students, with most at its Brooklyn campus. Graduate programs include a library and information science, architecture, and urban planning. Undergraduate programs include architecture, construction management, writing, critical and visual studies, industrial design and fine arts, totaling over 25 programs in all.

The [New York University Tandon School of Engineering](/wiki/New_York_University_Tandon_School_of_Engineering "New York University Tandon School of Engineering"), the United States' second oldest private [institute of technology](/wiki/Institute_of_technology "Institute of technology"), founded in 1854, has its main campus in Downtown's [MetroTech Center](/wiki/MetroTech_Center "MetroTech Center"), a commercial, civic and educational redevelopment project of which it was a key sponsor. NYU-Tandon is one of the 18 schools and colleges that comprise [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") (NYU).[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159)[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161)

[St. Francis College](/wiki/St._Francis_College "St. Francis College") is a Catholic college in Downtown Brooklyn founded in 1859 by Franciscan friars. Over 2,400 students attend the small liberal arts college. St. Francis is considered by *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* as one of the more diverse colleges, and was ranked one of the best baccalaureate colleges by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* magazine and *U.S. News & World Report*.[[161]](#cite_note-NYTimesDiversity-162)[[162]](#cite_note-Forbes.com-163)[[163]](#cite_note-USNWR-164)

Brooklyn also has smaller liberal arts institutions, such as [Saint Joseph's College](/wiki/St._Joseph%27s_College_(New_York) "St. Joseph's College (New York)") in Clinton Hill and [Boricua College](/wiki/Boricua_College "Boricua College") in [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn").

#### Community colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Community colleges")]

[Kingsborough Community College](/wiki/Kingsborough_Community_College "Kingsborough Community College") is a junior college in the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") system in [Manhattan Beach](/wiki/Manhattan_Beach,_Brooklyn "Manhattan Beach, Brooklyn").

#### Public Colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Public Colleges")]

[New York City College of Technology](/wiki/New_York_City_College_of_Technology "New York City College of Technology")(City Tech) is a public college in New York City. Founded in 1946, it is the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York")'s college of technology. Its main urban campus is located in Downtown Brooklyn.

## Brooklyn Public Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Brooklyn Public Library")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Brooklyn_Public_Library_by_DS.JPG/250px-Brooklyn_Public_Library_by_DS.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Public_Library_by_DS.JPG)

The Central Library at Grand Army Plaza

As an independent system, separate from the New York and Queens public library systems, the [Brooklyn Public Library](/wiki/Brooklyn_Public_Library "Brooklyn Public Library")[[164]](#cite_note-165) offers thousands of public programs, millions of books, and use of more than 850 free Internet-accessible computers. It also has books and periodicals in all the major languages spoken in Brooklyn, including English, Russian, Chinese, Spanish, Hebrew, and [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole"), as well as French, Yiddish, Hindi, Bengali, Polish, Italian, and Arabic. The Central Library is a landmarked building facing [Grand Army Plaza](/wiki/Grand_Army_Plaza "Grand Army Plaza").

There are 58 library branches, placing one within a half-mile of each Brooklyn resident. In addition to its specialized Business Library in Brooklyn Heights, the Library is preparing to construct its new Visual & Performing Arts Library (VPA) in the BAM Cultural District, which will focus on the link between new and emerging arts and technology and house traditional and digital collections. It will provide access and training to arts applications and technologies not widely available to the public. The collections will include the subjects of art, theater, dance, music, film, photography, and architecture. A special archive will house the records and history of Brooklyn's arts communities.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Public transport")]

See also: [Transportation in New York City](/wiki/Transportation_in_New_York_City "Transportation in New York City")

In 2015, about 57 percent of all households in Brooklyn were households without [automobiles](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile"). The citywide rate is 55 percent in New York City.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_Terminus.jpg/330px-Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_Terminus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Coney_Island-Stillwell_Avenue_Terminus.jpg)

The Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue subway station

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Atlantic_Terminal_LIRR_and_Subway_entrances.jpg/250px-Atlantic_Terminal_LIRR_and_Subway_entrances.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atlantic_Terminal_LIRR_and_Subway_entrances.jpg)

[Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal") is a major hub in Brooklyn.

Brooklyn features extensive [public transit](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport"). Nineteen [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") services, including the [Franklin Avenue Shuttle](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_Shuttle "Franklin Avenue Shuttle"), traverse the borough. Approximately 92.8% of Brooklyn residents traveling to Manhattan use the subway, despite the fact some neighborhoods like [Flatlands](/wiki/Flatlands,_Brooklyn "Flatlands, Brooklyn") and [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park,_Brooklyn "Marine Park, Brooklyn") are poorly served by subway service. Major stations, out of the [170 currently in Brooklyn](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_Subway_stations_in_Brooklyn "List of New York City Subway stations in Brooklyn"), include:

* [Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue%E2%80%93Barclays_Center_station "Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center station")
* [Broadway Junction](/wiki/Broadway_Junction_station "Broadway Junction station")
* [DeKalb Avenue](/wiki/DeKalb_Avenue_station_(BMT_lines) "DeKalb Avenue station (BMT lines)")
* [Jay Street–MetroTech](/wiki/Jay_Street%E2%80%93MetroTech_station "Jay Street–MetroTech station")
* [Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue](/wiki/Coney_Island%E2%80%93Stillwell_Avenue_station "Coney Island–Stillwell Avenue station")[[166]](#cite_note-167)

Proposed New York City Subway lines never built include a line along Nostrand or Utica Avenues to Marine Park,[[167]](#cite_note-168) as well as a subway line to [Spring Creek](/wiki/Spring_Creek,_Brooklyn "Spring Creek, Brooklyn").[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[169]](#cite_note-170)

Brooklyn was once served by [an extensive network of streetcars](/wiki/List_of_streetcar_lines_in_Brooklyn "List of streetcar lines in Brooklyn"), but many were replaced by the [public bus network](/wiki/List_of_bus_routes_in_Brooklyn "List of bus routes in Brooklyn") that covers the entire borough. There is also daily express bus service into Manhattan.[[170]](#cite_note-171) New York's famous yellow cabs also provide transportation in Brooklyn, although they are less numerous in the borough. There are three commuter rail stations in Brooklyn: [East New York](/wiki/East_New_York_(LIRR_station) "East New York (LIRR station)"), [Nostrand Avenue](/wiki/Nostrand_Avenue_(LIRR_station) "Nostrand Avenue (LIRR station)"), and [Atlantic Terminal](/wiki/Atlantic_Terminal "Atlantic Terminal"), the terminus of the [Atlantic Branch](/wiki/Atlantic_Branch "Atlantic Branch") of the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road"). The terminal is near the [Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue%E2%80%93Barclays_Center "Atlantic Avenue–Barclays Center") subway station, with ten connecting subway services.

In February 2015, Mayor [Bill de Blasio](/wiki/Bill_de_Blasio "Bill de Blasio") announced that the city government would begin a citywide ferry service called [NYC Ferry](/wiki/NYC_Ferry "NYC Ferry") to extend ferry transportation to communities in the city that have been traditionally underserved by public transit.[[171]](#cite_note-:0-172)[[172]](#cite_note-nbcny-hornblower-173) The ferry opened in May 2017,[[173]](#cite_note-nydailynews.com-174)[[174]](#cite_note-:14-175) with the Bay Ridge ferry serving southwestern Brooklyn and the [East River Ferry](/wiki/East_River_Ferry "East River Ferry") serving northwestern Brooklyn. A third route, the Rockaway ferry, makes one stop in the borough at [Brooklyn Army Terminal](/wiki/Brooklyn_Army_Terminal "Brooklyn Army Terminal").[[175]](#cite_note-auto-176)

A [streetcar](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar") line, the [Brooklyn–Queens Connector](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Queens_Connector "Brooklyn–Queens Connector"), was proposed by the city in February 2016,[[176]](#cite_note-177) with the planned timeline calling for service to begin around 2024.[[177]](#cite_note-178)

### Roadways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Roadways")]

See also: [Brooklyn streets](/wiki/Brooklyn_streets "Brooklyn streets") and [List of lettered Brooklyn avenues](/wiki/List_of_lettered_Brooklyn_avenues "List of lettered Brooklyn avenues")

The BQE between Red Hook and Brooklyn Heights

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Marine_Parkway_Bridge_2008_jeh_-_cropped.jpg/500px-Marine_Parkway_Bridge_2008_jeh_-_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marine_Parkway_Bridge_2008_jeh_-_cropped.jpg)

The [Marine Parkway Bridge](/wiki/Marine_Parkway_Bridge "Marine Parkway Bridge")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Williamsburg_Bridge_from_Gowanus_Bay_jeh.jpg/500px-Williamsburg_Bridge_from_Gowanus_Bay_jeh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Williamsburg_Bridge_from_Gowanus_Bay_jeh.jpg)

Williamsburg Bridge, as seen from [Wallabout Bay](/wiki/Wallabout_Bay "Wallabout Bay") with [Greenpoint](/wiki/Greenpoint,_Brooklyn "Greenpoint, Brooklyn") and Long Island City in background

Most of the [limited-access expressways and parkways](/wiki/Limited-access_road "Limited-access road") are in the western and southern sections of Brooklyn, where the borough's two [interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_Highway_System "Interstate Highway System") are located; [Interstate 278](/wiki/Interstate_278 "Interstate 278"), which uses the [Gowanus Expressway](/wiki/Gowanus_Expressway "Gowanus Expressway") and the [Brooklyn–Queens Expressway](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Queens_Expressway "Brooklyn–Queens Expressway"), traverses [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn") and [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights "Brooklyn Heights"), while [Interstate 478](/wiki/Interstate_478 "Interstate 478") is an unsigned route designation for the [Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel"), which connects to Manhattan.[[178]](#cite_note-179) Other prominent roadways are the [Prospect Expressway](/wiki/Prospect_Expressway "Prospect Expressway") ([New York State Route 27](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_27 "New York State Route 27")), the [Belt Parkway](/wiki/Belt_Parkway "Belt Parkway"), and the [Jackie Robinson Parkway](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson_Parkway "Jackie Robinson Parkway") (formerly the Interborough Parkway). Planned expressways that were never built include the Bushwick Expressway, an extension of [I-78](/wiki/I-78 "I-78")[[179]](#cite_note-180) and the Cross-Brooklyn Expressway, I-878.[[180]](#cite_note-181) Major thoroughfares include [Atlantic Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue_(New_York_City) "Atlantic Avenue (New York City)"), [Fourth Avenue](/wiki/Fourth_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Fourth Avenue (Brooklyn)"), 86th Street, [Kings Highway](/wiki/Kings_Highway_(Brooklyn) "Kings Highway (Brooklyn)"), [Bay Parkway](/wiki/Bay_Parkway_(Brooklyn) "Bay Parkway (Brooklyn)"), [Ocean Parkway](/wiki/Ocean_Parkway_(Brooklyn) "Ocean Parkway (Brooklyn)"), [Eastern Parkway](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway "Eastern Parkway"), [Linden Boulevard](/wiki/Linden_Boulevard "Linden Boulevard"), [McGuinness Boulevard](/wiki/McGuinness_Boulevard "McGuinness Boulevard"), [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue"), [Pennsylvania Avenue](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Avenue_(Brooklyn) "Pennsylvania Avenue (Brooklyn)"), and [Nostrand Avenue](/wiki/Nostrand_Avenue "Nostrand Avenue").

Much of Brooklyn has only named streets, but [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope "Park Slope"), [Bay Ridge](/wiki/Bay_Ridge "Bay Ridge"), Sunset Park, [Bensonhurst](/wiki/Bensonhurst "Bensonhurst"), and [Borough Park](/wiki/Borough_Park,_Brooklyn "Borough Park, Brooklyn") and the other western sections have [numbered streets](/wiki/List_of_numbered_Brooklyn_streets "List of numbered Brooklyn streets") running approximately northwest to southeast, and numbered avenues going approximately northeast to southwest. East of Dahill Road, lettered avenues (like Avenue M) run east and west, and numbered streets have the prefix "East". South of Avenue O, related numbered streets west of Dahill Road use the "West" designation.

This set of numbered streets ranges from West 37th Street to East 108 Street, and the avenues range from A–Z with names substituted for some of them in some neighborhoods (notably Albemarle, Beverley, Cortelyou, Dorchester, Ditmas, Foster, Farragut, Glenwood, Quentin). Numbered streets prefixed by "North" and "South" in Williamsburg, and "Bay", "Beach", "Brighton", "Plumb", "Paerdegat" or "Flatlands" along the southern and southwestern waterfront are loosely based on the old grids of the original towns of Kings County that eventually consolidated to form Brooklyn. These names often reflect the bodies of water or beaches around them, such as [Plumb Beach](/wiki/Plumb_Beach "Plumb Beach") or [Paerdegat Basin](/wiki/Paerdegat_Basin "Paerdegat Basin").

Brooklyn is connected to Manhattan by three bridges, the [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), and [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"); a vehicular tunnel, the Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel (also known as the Hugh L. Carey Tunnel); and several subway tunnels. The [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge") links Brooklyn with the more suburban borough of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"). Though much of its border is on land, Brooklyn shares several water crossings with [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), including the [Pulaski Bridge](/wiki/Pulaski_Bridge "Pulaski Bridge"), the [Greenpoint Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Greenpoint_Avenue_Bridge "Greenpoint Avenue Bridge"), the [Kosciuszko Bridge](/wiki/Kosciuszko_Bridge "Kosciuszko Bridge") (part of the Brooklyn–Queens Expressway), and the [Grand Street Bridge](/wiki/Grand_Street_Bridge "Grand Street Bridge"), all of which carry traffic over [Newtown Creek](/wiki/Newtown_Creek "Newtown Creek"), and the [Marine Parkway Bridge](/wiki/Marine_Parkway_Bridge "Marine Parkway Bridge") connecting Brooklyn to the [Rockaway Peninsula](/wiki/Rockaway_Peninsula "Rockaway Peninsula").

### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Waterways")]

Brooklyn was long a major shipping port, especially at the [Brooklyn Army Terminal](/wiki/Brooklyn_Army_Terminal "Brooklyn Army Terminal") and [Bush Terminal](/wiki/Bush_Terminal "Bush Terminal") in [Sunset Park](/wiki/Sunset_Park,_Brooklyn "Sunset Park, Brooklyn"). Most container ship cargo operations have shifted to the New Jersey side of New York Harbor, while the [Brooklyn Cruise Terminal](/wiki/Brooklyn_Cruise_Terminal "Brooklyn Cruise Terminal") in [Red Hook](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn") is a focal point for New York's growing cruise industry. The *[Queen Mary 2](/wiki/Queen_Mary_2 "Queen Mary 2")*, one of the [world's largest ocean liners](/wiki/List_of_largest_cruise_ships "List of largest cruise ships"), was designed specifically to fit under the Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge, the longest suspension bridge in the United States. She makes regular ports of call at the Red Hook terminal on her transatlantic crossings from [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton "Southampton"), England.[[175]](#cite_note-auto-176) The Brooklyn waterfront formerly employed tens of thousands of borough residents and acted as an incubator for industries across the entire city, and the decline of the port exacerbated Brooklyn's decline in the second half of the 20th century.

In February 2015, Mayor [Bill de Blasio](/wiki/Bill_de_Blasio "Bill de Blasio") announced that the city government would begin [NYC Ferry](/wiki/NYC_Ferry "NYC Ferry") to extend ferry transportation to traditionally underserved communities in the city.[[171]](#cite_note-:0-172)[[172]](#cite_note-nbcny-hornblower-173) The ferry opened in May 2017,[[173]](#cite_note-nydailynews.com-174)[[174]](#cite_note-:14-175) offering commuter services from the western shore of Brooklyn to Manhattan via three routes. The [East River Ferry](/wiki/East_River_Ferry "East River Ferry") serves points in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), [Long Island City](/wiki/Long_Island_City "Long Island City"), and northwestern Brooklyn via its East River route. The South Brooklyn and Rockaway routes serve southwestern Brooklyn before terminating in lower Manhattan. Ferries to Coney Island are also planned.[[175]](#cite_note-auto-176)

[NY Waterway](/wiki/NY_Waterway "NY Waterway") offers tours and charters. [SeaStreak](/wiki/SeaStreak "SeaStreak") also offers a weekday ferry service between the Brooklyn Army Terminal and the Manhattan ferry slips at [Pier 11/Wall Street](/wiki/Pier_11/Wall_Street "Pier 11/Wall Street") downtown and [East 34th Street Ferry Landing](/wiki/East_34th_Street_Ferry_Landing "East 34th Street Ferry Landing") in midtown. A [Cross-Harbor Rail Tunnel](/wiki/Cross-Harbor_Rail_Tunnel "Cross-Harbor Rail Tunnel"), originally proposed in the 1920s as a core project for the then-new [Port Authority of New York](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York "Port Authority of New York") is again being studied and discussed as a way to ease freight movements across a large swath of the metropolitan area.

Accessed July 6, 2025. "Henry Evelyn Pierrepont (1808-1888) conceived of the idea for Green-Wood Cemetery in the early 1830s, envisioning an area amid the picturesque hills of Brooklyn, to serve those in the New York City area with natural and serene burial space. By 1838, Pierrepont's vision finally became a reality. On April 18, 1838, The New York State Legislature passed an Act of Incorporation, declaring that 'The Green-Wood Cemetery' was established 'for the purpose of establishing a public burial ground in the City of Brooklyn.'."
